rows.pali: 294.Idāni āruppānantaraṃ ekā saññāti evaṃ uddiṭṭhāya āhāre paṭikkūlasaññāya bhāvanāniddeso anuppatto.
rows.english: 1. Now comes the description of the development of the perception of repulsiveness in nutriment, which was listed as the “one perception”1 next to the immaterial states (III.105).

rows.pali: Tattha āharatīti āhāro.
rows.english: Herein, it nourishes (āharati, lit. “brings on”), thus it is nutriment (āhāra, lit. “bringing on”).

rows.pali: So catubbidho kabaḷīkārāhāro, phassāhāro, manosañcetanāhāro, viññāṇāhāroti.
rows.english: That is of four kinds as: physical nutriment, nutriment consisting of contact, nutriment consisting of mental volition, and nutriment consisting of consciousness.2

rows.pali: Ko panettha kimāharatīti?
rows.english: 2. But what is it here that nourishes (brings on) what?

rows.pali: Kabaḷīkārāhāro ojaṭṭhamakaṃ rūpaṃ āharati.
rows.english: Physical nutriment (kabaliṅkārāhāra) nourishes (brings on) the materiality of the octad that has nutritive essence as eighth:3

rows.pali: Phassāhāro tisso vedanā āharati.
rows.english: contact as nutriment nourishes (brings on) the three kinds of feeling;

rows.pali: Manosañcetanāhāro tīsu bhavesu paṭisandhiṃ āharati.
rows.english: mental volition as nutriment nourishes (brings on) rebirth- linking in the three kinds of becoming;

rows.pali: Viññāṇāhāro paṭisandhikkhaṇe nāmarūpaṃ āharati.
rows.english: consciousness as nutriment nourishes (brings on) mentality-materiality at the moment of rebirth-linking.

rows.pali: Tesu kabaḷīkārāhāre nikantibhayaṃ.
rows.english: 3. Now, when there is physical nutriment there is attachment, which brings peril;

rows.pali: Phassāhāre upagamanabhayaṃ.
rows.english: when there is nutriment as contact there is approaching, which brings peril;

rows.pali: Manosañcetanāhāre upapattibhayaṃ.
rows.english: when there is nutriment as mental volition there is rebirth-linking, which brings peril.4

rows.pali: Viññāṇāhāre paṭisandhibhayaṃ.
Evaṃ sappaṭibhayesu ca tesu kabaḷīkārāhāro puttamaṃsūpamena (saṃ. ni. 2.63) dīpetabbo.
rows.english: And to show how they bring fear thus, physical nutriment should be illustrated by the simile of the child’s flesh (S II 98),

rows.pali: Phassāhāro niccammagāvūpamena (saṃ. ni. 2.63).
rows.english: contact as nutriment by the simile of the hideless cow (S II 99),

rows.pali: Manosañcetanāhāro aṅgārakāsūpamena (saṃ. ni. 2.63).
rows.english: mental volition as nutriment by the simile of the pit of live coals (S II 99),

rows.pali: Viññāṇāhāro sattisatūpamenāti (saṃ. ni. 2.63).
rows.english: and consciousness as nutriment by the simile of the hundred spears (S II 100).

rows.pali: Imesu pana catūsu āhāresu asitapītakhāyitasāyitappabhedo kabaḷīkāro āhārova imasmiṃ atthe āhāroti adhippeto.
rows.english: 4.But of these four kinds of nutriment it is only physical nutriment, classed as what is eaten, drunk, chewed, and tasted, that is intended here as “nutriment” in this sense.

rows.pali: Tasmiṃ āhāre paṭikkūlākāraggahaṇavasena uppannā saññā āhāre paṭikkūlasaññā.
rows.english: The perception arisen as the apprehension of the repulsive aspect in that nutriment is, “perception of repulsiveness in nutriment.”

rows.pali: Taṃ āhāre paṭikkūlasaññaṃ bhāvetukāmena kammaṭṭhānaṃ uggahetvā uggahato ekapadampi avirajjhantena rahogatena paṭisallīnena asitapītakhāyitasāyitappabhede kabaḷīkārāhāre dasahākārehi paṭikkūlatā paccavekkhitabbā.
rows.english: 5. One who wants to develop that perception of repulsiveness in nutriment should learn the meditation subject and see that he has no uncertainty about even a single word of what he has learnt. Then he should go into solitary retreat and review repulsiveness in ten aspects in the physical nutriment

rows.pali: Seyyathidaṃ, gamanato, pariyesanato, paribhogato, āsayato, nidhānato, aparipakkato, paripakkato, phalato, nissandato, sammakkhanatoti.
rows.english: classified as what is eaten, drunk, chewed, and tasted, that is to say, as to going, seeking, using, secretion, receptacle, what is uncooked (undigested), what is cooked (digested), fruit, outflow, and smearing.

rows.pali: 295.Tattha gamanatoti evaṃ mahānubhāve nāma sāsane pabbajitena sakalarattiṃ buddhavacanasajjhāyaṃ vā samaṇadhammaṃ vā katvā kālasseva vuṭṭhāya cetiyaṅgaṇabodhiyaṅgaṇavattaṃ katvā pānīyaṃ paribhojanīyaṃ upaṭṭhapetvā pariveṇaṃ sammajjitvā sarīraṃ paṭijaggitvā āsanamārūyha vīsatiṃsa vāre kammaṭṭhānaṃ manasikaritvā uṭṭhāya pattacīvaraṃ gahetvā nijanasambādhāni pavivekasukhāni chāyūdakasampannāni sucīni sītalāni ramaṇīyabhūmibhāgāni tapovanāni pahāya ariyaṃ vivekaratiṃ anapekkhitvā susānābhimukhena siṅgālena viya āhāratthāya gāmābhimukhena gantabbaṃ.
rows.english: 6. 1. Herein, as to going: even when a man has gone forth in so mighty a dispensation, still after he has perhaps spent all night reciting the Enlightened One’s word or doing the ascetic’ s work, after he has risen early to do the duties connected with the shrine terrace and the Enlightenment-tree terrace, to set out the water for drinking and washing, to sweep the grounds and to see to the needs of the body, after he has sat down on his seat and given attention to his meditation subject twenty or thirty times5 and got up again, then he must take his bowl and [outer] robe, he must leave behind the ascetics’ woods that are not crowded with people, offer the bliss of seclusion, possess shade and water, and are clean, cool, delightful places, he must disregard the Noble Ones’ delight in seclusion, and he must set out for the village in order to get nutriment, as a jackal for the charnel ground.

rows.pali: Evaṃ gacchatā ca mañcamhā vā pīṭhamhā vā otaraṇato paṭṭhāya pādarajagharagolikavaccādisamparikiṇṇaṃ paccattharaṇaṃ akkamitabbaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 7.And as he goes thus, from the time when he steps down from his bed or chair he has to tread on a carpet6 covered with the dust of his feet, geckos’ droppings, and so on.

rows.pali: Tato appekadā mūsikajatukavaccādīhi upahatattā antogabbhato paṭikkūlataraṃ pamukhaṃ daṭṭhabbaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Next he has to see the doorstep,7 which is more repulsive than the inside of the room since it is often fouled with the droppings of rats, bats,8 and so on.

rows.pali: Tato ulūkapārāvatādivaccasammakkhitattā uparimatalato paṭikkūlataraṃ heṭṭhimatalaṃ.
rows.english: Next the lower terrace, which is more repulsive than the terrace above since it is all smeared with the droppings of owls, pigeons,9 and so on.

rows.pali: Tato kadāci kadāci vāteritehi purāṇatiṇapaṇṇehi gilānasāmaṇerānaṃ muttakarīsakheḷasiṅghāṇikāhi vassakāle udakacikkhallādīhi ca saṃkiliṭṭhattā heṭṭhimatalato paṭikkūlataraṃ pariveṇaṃ.
rows.english: Next the grounds,10 which are more repulsive than the lower floor since they are defiled by old grass and leaves blown about by the wind, by sick novices’ urine, excrement, spittle and snot, and in the rainy season by water, mud, and so on.

rows.pali: Pariveṇato paṭikkūlatarā vihāraracchā daṭṭhabbā hoti.
rows.english: And he has to see the road to the monastery, which is more repulsive than the grounds.

rows.pali: Anupubbena pana bodhiñca cetiyañca vanditvā vitakkamāḷake ṭhitena muttarāsisadisaṃ cetiyaṃ morapiñchakalāpamanoharaṃ bodhiṃ devavimānasampattisassirīkaṃ senāsanañca anapaloketvā evarūpaṃ nāma ramaṇīyaṃ padesaṃ piṭṭhito katvā āhārahetu gantabbaṃ bhavissatīti pakkamitvā gāmamaggaṃ paṭipannena khāṇukaṇṭakamaggopi udakavegabhinnavisamamaggopi daṭṭhabbo hoti.
rows.english: 8.In due course, after standing in the debating lodge11 when he has finished paying homage at the Enlightenment Tree and the shrine, he sets out thinking, “Instead of looking at the shrine that is like a cluster of pearls, and the Enlightenment Tree that is as lovely as a bouquet of peacock’s tail feathers, and the abode that is as fair as a god’s palace, I must now turn my back on such a charming place and go abroad for the sake of food;” and on the way to the village, the view of a road of stumps and thorns and an uneven road broken up by the force of water awaits him.

rows.pali: Tato gaṇḍaṃ paṭicchādentena viya nivāsanaṃ nivāsetvā vaṇacoḷakaṃ bandhantena viya kāyabandhanaṃ bandhitvā aṭṭhisaṅghātaṃ paṭicchādentena viya cīvaraṃ pārupitvā bhesajjakapālaṃ nīharantena viya pattaṃ nīharitvā gāmadvārasamīpaṃ pāpuṇantena hatthikuṇapaassakuṇapagokuṇapamahiṃsakuṇapamanussakuṇapaahikuṇapakukkurakuṇapānipi daṭṭhabbāni bhavanti.
rows.english: 9.Next, after he has put on his waist cloth as one who hides an abscess, and tied his waist band as one who ties a bandage on a wound, and robed himself in his upper robes as one who hides a skeleton, and taken out his bowl as one who takes out a pan for medicine, when he reaches the vicinity of the village gate, perhaps the sight of an elephant’s carcass, a horse’s carcass, a buffalo’s carcass, a human carcass, a snake’s carcass, or a dog’s carcass awaits him,

rows.pali: Na kevalañca daṭṭhabbāni, gandhopi nesaṃ ghānaṃ paṭihanamāno adhivāsetabbo hoti.
rows.english: and not only that, but he has to suffer his nose to be assailed by the smell of them.

rows.pali: Tato gāmadvāre ṭhatvā caṇḍahatthiassādiparissayaparivajjanatthaṃ gāmaracchā oloketabbā honti.
rows.english: Next, as he stands in the village gateway, he must scan the village streets in order to avoid danger from savage elephants, horses, and so on.

rows.pali: Iccetaṃ paccattharaṇādianekakuṇapapariyosānaṃ paṭikkūlaṃ āhārahetu akkamitabbañca daṭṭhabbañca ghāyitabbañca hoti.
rows.english: 10. So this repulsive [experience] beginning with the carpet that has to be trodden on and ending with the various kinds of carcasses that have to be seen and smelled, [has to be undergone] for the sake of nutriment:

rows.pali: Aho vata bho paṭikkūlo āhāroti evaṃ gamanato paṭikkūlatā paccavekkhitabbā.
rows.english: “Oh, nutriment is indeed a repulsive thing!” This is how repulsiveness should be reviewed as to going.

rows.pali: 296.Kathaṃ pariyesanato?
rows.english: 11.2. How as to seeking?

rows.pali: Evaṃ gamanapaṭikkūlaṃ adhivāsetvāpi gāmaṃ paviṭṭhena saṅghāṭipārutena kapaṇamanussena viya kapālahatthena gharapaṭipāṭiyā gāmavīthīsu caritabbaṃ hoti.
rows.english: When he has endured the repulsiveness of going in this way, and has gone into the village, and is clothed in his cloak of patches, he has to wander in the village streets from house to house like a beggar with a dish in his hand.

rows.pali: Yattha vassakāle akkantaakkantaṭṭhāne yāva piṇḍikamaṃsāpi udakacikkhalle pādā pavisanti, ekena hatthena pattaṃ gahetabbaṃ hoti, ekena cīvaraṃ ukkhipitabbaṃ.
rows.english: And in the rainy season wherever he treads his feet sink into water and mire up to the flesh of the calves.12 He has to hold the bowl in one hand and his robe up with the other.

rows.pali: Gimhakāle vātavegena samuṭṭhitehi paṃsutiṇarajehi okiṇṇasarīrena caritabbaṃ.
rows.english: In the hot season he has to go about with his body covered with the dirt, grass, and dust blown about by the wind.

rows.pali: Taṃ taṃ gehadvāraṃ patvā macchadhovanamaṃsadhovanataṇḍuladhovanakheḷasiṅghāṇikasunakhasūkaravaccādīhi sammissāni kimikulākulāni nīlamakkhikaparikiṇṇāni oḷigallāni ceva candanikaṭṭhānāni ca daṭṭhabbāni honti akkamitabbānipi.
rows.english: On reaching such and such a house door he has to see and even to tread in gutters and cesspools covered with blue-bottles and seething with all the species of worms, all mixed up with fish washings, meat washings, rice washings, spittle, snot, dogs’ and pigs’ excrement, and what not,

rows.pali: Yato tā makkhikā uṭṭhahitvā saṅghāṭiyampi pattepi sīsepi nilīyanti.
rows.english: from which flies come up and settle on his outer cloak of patches and on his bowl and on his head.

rows.pali: Gharaṃ paviṭṭhassāpi keci denti, keci na denti.
rows.english: 12.And when he enters a house, some give and some do not.

rows.pali: Dadamānāpi ekacce hiyyo pakkabhattampi purāṇakhajjakampi pūtikummāsapūpādīnipi dadanti.
rows.english: And when they give, some give yesterday’s cooked rice and stale cakes and rancid jelly, sauce and so on.13

rows.pali: Adadamānāpi kecideva "aticchatha, bhante"ti vadanti, keci pana apassamānā viya tuṇhī honti, keci aññena mukhaṃ karonti, keci "gaccha, re muṇḍakā"tiādīhi pharusavācāhi samudācaranti.
rows.english: Some, not giving, say, “Please pass on, venerable sir,” others keep silent as if they did not see him. Some avert their faces. Others treat him with harsh words such as: “Go away, you bald-head.”

rows.pali: Evaṃ kapaṇamanussena viya gāme piṇḍāya caritvā nikkhamitabbanti.
rows.english: When he has wandered for alms in the village in this way like a beggar, he has to depart from it.

rows.pali: Iccetaṃ gāmappavesanato paṭṭhāya yāva nikkhamanā udakacikkhallādipaṭikkūlaṃ āhārahetu akkamitabbañceva daṭṭhabbañca adhivāsetabbañca hoti.
rows.english: 13.So this [experience] beginning with the entry into the village and ending with the departure from it, which is repulsive owing to the water, mud, etc., that has to be trodden in and seen and endured, [has to be undergone] for the sake of nutriment:

rows.pali: Aho vata bho paṭikkūlo āhāroti evaṃ pariyesanato paṭikkūlatā paccavekkhitabbā.
rows.english: “Oh, nutriment is indeed a repulsive thing!” This is how repulsiveness should be reviewed as to seeking.

rows.pali: 297.Kathaṃ paribhogato?
rows.english: 14. 3. How as to using?

rows.pali: Evaṃ pariyiṭṭhāhārena pana bahigāme phāsukaṭṭhāne sukhanisinnena yāva tattha hatthaṃ na otāreti, tāva tathārūpaṃ garuṭṭhāniyaṃ bhikkhuṃ vā lajjimanussaṃ vā disvā nimantetumpi sakkā hoti.
rows.english: After he has sought the nutriment in this way and is sitting at ease in a comfortable place outside the village, then so long as he has not dipped his hand into it he would be able to invite a respected bhikkhu or a decent person, if he saw one, [to share it];

rows.pali: Bhuñjitukāmatāya panettha hatthe otāritamatte "gaṇhathā"ti vadantena lajjitabbaṃ hoti.
rows.english: but as soon as he has dipped his hand into it out of desire to eat he would be ashamed to say, “Take some.”

rows.pali: Hatthaṃ pana otāretvā maddantassa pañcaṅgulianusārena sedo paggharamāno sukkhathaddhabhattampi temento muduṃ karoti.
rows.english: And when he has dipped his hand in and is squeezing it up, the sweat trickling down his five fingers wets any dry crisp food there may be and makes it sodden.

rows.pali: Atha tasmiṃ parimaddanamattenāpi sambhinnasobhe ālopaṃ katvā mukhe ṭhapite heṭṭhimadantā udukkhalakiccaṃ sādhenti, uparimā musalakiccaṃ, jivhā hatthakiccaṃ.
rows.english: 15.And when its good appearance has been spoilt by his squeezing it up, and it has been made into a ball and put into his mouth, then the lower teeth function as a mortar, the upper teeth as a pestle, and the tongue as a hand.

rows.pali: Taṃ tattha suvānadoṇiyaṃ suvānapiṇḍamiva dantamusalehi koṭṭetvā jivhāya samparivattiyamānaṃ jivhāgge tanupasannakheḷo makkheti, vemajjhato paṭṭhāya bahalakheḷo makkheti, dantakaṭṭhena asampattaṭṭhāne dantagūthako makkheti.
rows.english: It gets pounded there with the pestle of the teeth like a dog’s dinner in a dog’s trough, while he turns it over and over with his tongue; then the thin spittle at the tip of the tongue smears it, and the thick spittle behind the middle of the tongue smears it, and the filth from the teeth in the parts where a tooth-stick cannot reach smears it.

rows.pali: So evaṃ vicuṇṇitamakkhito taṅkhaṇaññeva antarahitavaṇṇagandhasaṅkhāraviseso suvānadoṇiyaṃ ṭhitasuvānavamathu viya paramajegucchabhāvaṃ upagacchati.
rows.english: 16. When thus mashed up and besmeared, this peculiar compound now destitute of the [original] colour and smell is reduced to a condition as utterly nauseating as a dog’s vomit in a dog’s trough.

rows.pali: Evarūpopi samāno cakkhussa āpāthaṃ atītattā ajjhoharitabbo hotīti evaṃ paribhogato paṭikkūlatā paccavekkhitabbā.
rows.english: Yet, notwithstanding that it is like this, it can still be swallowed because it is no longer in range of the eye’s focus. This is how repulsiveness should be reviewed as to using.

rows.pali: 298.Kathaṃ āsayato?
rows.english: 17.4. How as to secretion?

rows.pali: Evaṃ paribhogaṃ upagato ca panesa anto pavisamāno yasmā buddhapaccekabuddhānampi raññopi cakkavattissa pittasemhapubbalohitāsayesu catūsu aññataro āsayo hotiyeva.
rows.english: Buddhas and Paccekabuddhas and Wheel-turning Monarchs have only one of the four secretions consisting of bile, phlegm, pus and blood,

rows.pali: Mandapuññānaṃ pana cattāro āsayā honti.
rows.english: but those with weak merit have all four.

rows.pali: Tasmā yassa pittāsayo adhiko hoti, tassa bahalamadhukatelamakkhito viya paramajeguccho hoti.
rows.english: So when [the food] has arrived at the stage of being eaten and it enters inside, then in one whose secretion of bile is in excess it becomes as utterly nauseating as if smeared with thick madhuka oil;

rows.pali: Yassa semhāsayo adhiko hoti, tassa nāgabalapaṇṇarasamakkhito viya.
rows.english: in one whose secretion of phlegm in excess it is as if smeared with the juice of nāgabalā leaves;14

rows.pali: Yassa pubbāsayo adhiko hoti, tassa pūtitakkamakkhito viya.
rows.english: in one whose secretion of pus is in excess it is as if smeared with rancid buttermilk;

rows.pali: Yassa lohitāsayo adhiko hoti, tassa rajanamakkhito viya paramajeguccho hotīti evaṃ āsayato paṭikkūlatā paccavekkhitabbā.
rows.english: and in one whose secretion of blood is in excess it is as utterly nauseating as if smeared with dye. This is how repulsiveness should be reviewed as to secretion.

rows.pali: 299.Kathaṃ nidhānato?
rows.english: 18. 5. How as to receptacle?

rows.pali: So imesu catūsu āsayesu aññatarena āsayena makkhito antoudaraṃ pavisitvā neva suvaṇṇabhājane na maṇirajatādibhājanesu nidhānaṃ gacchati.
rows.english: When it has gone inside the belly and is smeared with one of these secretions, then the receptacle it goes into is no gold dish or crystal or silver dish and so on.

rows.pali: Sace pana dasavassikena ajjhohariyati dasa vassāni adhotavaccakūpasadise okāse patiṭṭhahati.
rows.english: On the contrary, if it is swallowed by one ten years old, it finds itself in a place like a cesspit unwashed for ten years.

rows.pali: Sace vīsa, tiṃsa, cattālīsa, paññāsa, saṭṭhi, sattati, asīti, navutivassikena, sace vassasatikena ajjhohariyati.
rows.english: If it is swallowed by one twenty years old, thirty, forty, fifty, sixty, seventy, eighty, ninety ears old,

rows.pali: Vassasataṃ adhotavaccakūpasadise okāse patiṭṭhahatīti evaṃ nidhānato paṭikkūlatā paccavekkhitabbā.
rows.english: if it is swallowed by one a hundred years old, it finds itself in a place like a cesspit unwashed for a hundred years. This is how repulsiveness should be reviewed as to receptacle.

rows.pali: 300.Kathaṃ aparipakkato?
rows.english: 19. 6. How as to what is uncooked (undigested)?

rows.pali: So panāyamāhāro evarūpe okāse nidhānamupagato yāva aparipakko hoti, tāva tasmiññeva yathāvuttappakāre paramandhakāratimise nānākuṇapagandhavāsitapavanavicarite atiduggandhajegucche padese yathā nāma nidāghe akālameghena abhivuṭṭhamhi caṇḍālagāmadvāraāvāṭe patitāni tiṇapaṇṇakilañjakhaṇḍaahikukkuramanussakuṇapādīni sūriyātapena santattāni pheṇapupphuḷakācitāni tiṭṭhanti, evameva taṃdivasampi hiyyopi tato purime divasepi ajjhohato sabbo ekato hutvā semhapaṭalapariyonaddho kāyaggisantāpakuthitakuthanasañjātapheṇapupphuḷakācito paramajegucchabhāvaṃ upagantvā tiṭṭhatīti evaṃ aparipakkato paṭikkūlatā paccavekkhitabbā.
rows.english: After this nutriment has arrived at such a place for its receptacle, then for as long as it remains uncooked it stays in that same place just described, which is shrouded in absolute darkness, pervaded by draughts,15 tainted by various smells of ordure and utterly fetid and loathsome. And just as when a cloud out of season has rained during a drought and bits of grass and leaves and rushes and the carcasses of snakes, dogs and human beings that have collected in a pit at the gate of an outcaste village remain there warmed by the sun’s heat until the pit becomes covered with froth and bubbles, so too, what has been swallowed that day and yesterday and the day before remains there together, and being smothered by the layer of phlegm and covered with froth and bubbles produced by digestion through being fermented by the heat of the bodily fires, it becomes quite loathsome. This is how repulsiveness should be reviewed as to what is uncooked.

rows.pali: 301.Kathaṃ paripakkato?
rows.english: 20. 7. How as to what is cooked?

rows.pali: So tattakāyagginā paripakko samāno na suvaṇṇarajatādidhātuyo viya suvaṇṇarajatādibhāvaṃ upagacchati.
rows.english: When it has been completely cooked there by the bodily fires, it does not turn into gold, silver, etc., as the ores16 of gold, silver, etc., do [through smelting].

rows.pali: Pheṇapupphuḷake pana muñcanto saṇhakaraṇiyaṃ pisitvā nāḷike pakkhittapaṇḍumattikā viya karīsabhāvaṃ upagantvā pakkāsayaṃ, muttabhāvaṃ upagantvā muttavatthiñca pūretīti evaṃ paripakkato paṭikkūlatā paccavekkhitabbā.
rows.english: Instead, giving off froth and bubbles, it turns into excrement and fills the receptacle for digested food, like brown clay squeezed with a smoothing trowel and packed into a tube, and it turns into urine and fills the bladder. This is how repulsiveness should be reviewed as to what is cooked.

rows.pali: 302.Kathaṃ phalato?
rows.english: 21. 8. How as to fruit?

rows.pali: Sammā paripaccamāno ca panāyaṃ kesalomanakhadantādīni nānākuṇapāni nipphādeti asammāparipaccamāno daddukaṇḍukacchukuṭṭhakilāsasosakāsātisārappabhutīni rogasatāni, idamassa phalanti evaṃ phalato paṭikkūlatā paccavekkhitabbā.
rows.english: When it has been rightly cooked, it produces the various kinds of ordure consisting of head hairs, body hairs, nails, teeth, and the rest. When wrongly cooked it produces the hundred diseases beginning with itch, ring-worm, smallpox, leprosy, plague, consumption, coughs, flux, and so on. Such is its fruit. This is how repulsiveness should be reviewed as to fruit.

rows.pali: 303.Kathaṃ nissandato?
rows.english: 22.9. How as to outflow?

rows.pali: Ajjhohariyamāno cesa ekena dvārena pavisitvā nissandamāno akkhimhā akkhigūthako kaṇṇamhā kaṇṇagūthakotiādinā pakārena anekehi dvārehi nissandati.
rows.english: On being swallowed, it enters by one door, after which it flows out by several doors in the way beginning, “Eye-dirt from the eye, ear- dirt from the ear” (Sn 197).

rows.pali: Ajjhoharaṇasamaye cesa mahāparivārenāpi ajjhohariyati.
rows.english: And on being swallowed it is swallowed even in the company of large gatherings.

rows.pali: Nissandanasamaye pana uccārapassāvādibhāvaṃ upagato ekakeneva nīhariyati.
rows.english: But on flowing out, now converted into excrement, urine, etc., it is excreted only in solitude.

rows.pali: Paṭhamadivase ca naṃ paribhuñjanto haṭṭhapahaṭṭhopi hoti udaggudaggo pītisomanassajāto.
rows.english: On the first day one is delighted to eat it, elated and full of happiness and joy.

rows.pali: Dutiyadivase nissandento pihitanāsiko hoti vikuṇitamukho jegucchī maṅkubhūto.
rows.english: On the second day one stops one’s nose to void it, with a wry face, disgusted and dismayed.

rows.pali: Paṭhamadivase ca naṃ ratto giddho gadhito mucchitopi ajjhoharitvā dutiyadivase ekarattivāsena viratto aṭṭīyamāno harāyamāno jigucchamāno nīharati.
rows.english: And on the first day one swallows it lustfully, greedily, gluttonously, infatuatedly. But on the second day, after a single night has passed, one excretes it with distaste, ashamed, humiliated and disgusted.

rows.pali: Tenāhu porāṇā –
rows.english: Hence the Ancients said:

rows.pali: Evaṃ nissandato paṭikkūlatā paccavekkhitabbā.
rows.english: This is how repulsiveness should be reviewed as to outflow.

rows.pali: 304.Kathaṃ sammakkhanato?
rows.english: 24.10. How as to smearing?

rows.pali: Paribhogakālepi cesa hatthaoṭṭhajivhātālūni sammakkheti.
rows.english: At the time of using it he smears his hands, lips, tongue and palate,

rows.pali: Tāni tena sammakkhitattā paṭikkūlāni honti, yāni dhotānipi gandhaharaṇatthaṃ punappunaṃ dhovitabbāni honti.
rows.english: and they become repulsive by being smeared with it. And even when washed, they have to be washed again and again in order to remove the smell.

rows.pali: Paribhutto samāno yathā nāma odane paccamāne thusakaṇakuṇḍakādīni uttaritvā ukkhalimukhavaṭṭipidhāniyo makkhanti, evameva sakalasarīrānugatena kāyagginā pheṇuddehakaṃ paccitvā uttaramāno dante dantamalabhāvena sammakkheti.
rows.english: And, just as, when rice is being boiled, the husks, the red powder covering the grain, etc., rise up and smear the mouth, rim and lid of the cauldron, so too, when eaten it rises up during its cooking and simmering by the bodily fire that pervades the whole body, it turns into tartar, which smears the teeth,

rows.pali: Jivhātāluppabhutīni kheḷasemhādibhāvena, akkhikaṇṇanāsaadhomaggādike akkhigūthakakaṇṇagūthakasiṅghāṇikāmuttakarīsādibhāvena sammakkheti.
rows.english: and it turns into spittle, phlegm, etc., which respectively smear the tongue, palate, etc.; and it turns into eye-dirt, ear-dirt, snot, urine, excrement, etc., which respectively smear the eyes, ears, nose and nether passages.

rows.pali: Yena sammakkhitāni imāni dvārāni divase divase dhoviyamānānipi neva sucīni, na manoramāni honti.
rows.english: And when these doors are smeared by it, they never become either clean or pleasing even though washed every day.

rows.pali: Yesu ekaccaṃ dhovitvā hattho puna udakena dhovitabbo hoti.
rows.english: And after one has washed a certain one of these, the hand has to be washed again.17

rows.pali: Ekaccaṃ dhovitvā dvattikkhattuṃ gomayenapi mattikāyapi gandhacuṇṇenapi dhovato pāṭikulyatā vigacchatīti evaṃ sammakkhanato paṭikkūlatā paccavekkhitabbā.
rows.english: And after one has washed a certain one of these, the repulsiveness does not depart from it even after two or three washings with cow dung and clay and scented powder. This is how repulsiveness should be reviewed as to smearing.

rows.pali: 305.Tassevaṃ dasahākārehi paṭikkūlataṃ paccavekkhato takkāhataṃ vitakkāhataṃ karontassa paṭikkūlākāravasena kabaḷīkārāhāro pākaṭo hoti.
rows.english: 25.As he reviews repulsiveness in this way in ten aspects and strikes at it with thought and applied thought, physical nutriment becomes evident to him in its repulsive aspect.

rows.pali: So taṃ nimittaṃ punappunaṃ āsevati bhāveti bahulīkaroti.
rows.english: He cultivates that sign18 again and again, develops and repeatedly practices it.

rows.pali: Tassevaṃ karoto nīvaraṇāni vikkhambhanti.
rows.english: As he does so, the hindrances are suppressed,

rows.pali: Kabaḷīkārāhārassa sabhāvadhammatāya gambhīrattā appanaṃ appattena upacārasamādhinā cittaṃ samādhiyati.
rows.english: and his mind is concentrated in access concentration, but without reaching absorption because of the profundity of physical nutriment as a state with an individual essence.

rows.pali: Paṭikkūlākāraggahaṇavasena panettha saññā pākaṭā hoti.
rows.english: But perception is evident here in the apprehension of the repulsive aspect,

rows.pali: Tasmā imaṃ kammaṭṭhānaṃ āhāre paṭikkūlasaññā icceva saṅkhaṃ gacchati.
rows.english: which is why this meditation subject goes by the name of “perception of repulsiveness in nutriment.”

rows.pali: Imañca pana āhāre paṭikkūlasaññaṃ anuyuttassa bhikkhuno rasataṇhāya cittaṃ patilīyati patikuṭati pativaṭṭati.
rows.english: 26. When a bhikkhu devotes himself to this perception of repulsiveness in nutriment, his mind retreats, retracts and recoils from craving for flavours.

rows.pali: So kantāranittharaṇatthiko viya puttamaṃsaṃ vigatamado āhāraṃ āhāreti yāvadeva dukkhassa nittharaṇatthāya.
rows.english: He nourishes himself with nutriment without vanity and only for the purpose of crossing over suffering, as one who seeks to cross over the desert eats his own dead child’s flesh (S II 98).

rows.pali: Athassa appakasireneva kabaḷīkārāhārapariññāmukhena pañcakāmaguṇiko rāgo pariññaṃ gacchati.
rows.english: Then his greed for the five cords of sense desire comes to be fully understood without difficulty by means of the full understanding of the physical nutriment.

rows.pali: So pañcakāmaguṇapariññāmukhena rūpakkhandhaṃ parijānāti.
rows.english: He fully understands the materiality aggregate by means of the full-understanding of the five cords of sense desire.

rows.pali: Aparipakkādipaṭikkūlabhāvavasena cassa kāyagatāsatibhāvanāpi pāripūriṃ gacchati, asubhasaññāya anulomapaṭipadaṃ paṭipanno hoti.
rows.english: Development of mindfulness occupied with the body comes to perfection in him through the repulsiveness of “what is uncooked” and the rest. He has entered upon a way that is in conformity with the perception of foulness.

rows.pali: Imaṃ pana paṭipattiṃ nissāya diṭṭheva dhamme amatapariyosānataṃ anabhisambhuṇanto sugatiparāyano hotīti.
rows.english: And by keeping to this way, even if he does not experience the deathless goal in this life, he is at least bound for a happy destiny.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ āhāre paṭikkūlasaññābhāvanāya vitthārakathā.
rows.english: This is the detailed explanation of the development of the perception of repulsiveness in nutriment.

rows.pali: 306.Idāni āhāre paṭikkūlasaññānantaraṃ ekaṃ vavatthānanti evaṃ uddiṭṭhassa catudhātuvavatthānassa bhāvanāniddeso anuppatto.
rows.english: [WORD DEFINITIONS] 27.Now comes the description of the development of the definition of the four elements, which was listed as the “one defining” next to the perception of repulsiveness in nutriment (III.105).

rows.pali: Tattha vavatthānanti sabhāvūpalakkhaṇavasena sanniṭṭhānaṃ, catunnaṃ dhātūnaṃ vavatthānaṃ catudhātuvavatthānaṃ.
rows.english: Herein, “defining” (vavatthāna) is determining by characterizing individual essences. 19 [The compound] catudhātuvavatthāna (“four-element defining”) is [resolvable into] catunnaṃ dhātūnaṃ vavatthānaṃ (“defining of the four elements”).

rows.pali: Dhātumanasikāro, dhātukammaṭṭhānaṃ, catudhātuvavatthānanti atthato ekaṃ.
rows.english: “Attention given to elements,” “the meditation subject consisting of elements” and “defining of the four elements” all mean the same thing.

rows.pali: Tayidaṃ dvidhā āgataṃ saṅkhepato ca vitthārato ca.
rows.english: This is given in two ways: in brief and in detail.

rows.pali: Saṅkhepato mahāsatipaṭṭhāne āgataṃ.
rows.english: It is given in brief in the Mahāsatipaṭṭhāna Sutta (D II 294),

rows.pali: Vitthārato mahāhatthipadūpame rāhulovāde dhātuvibhaṅge ca.
rows.english: and in detail in the Mahāhatthipadopama Sutta (M I 185), the Rāhulovāda Sutta (M I 421), and the Dhātuvibhaṅga Sutta (M III 240).

rows.pali: Tañhi –

"Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, dakkho goghātako vā goghātakantevāsī vā gāviṃ vadhitvā catumahāpathe bilaso vibhajitvā nisinno assa, evameva kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu imameva kāyaṃ yathāṭhitaṃ yathāpaṇihitaṃ dhātuso paccavekkhati, atthi imasmiṃ kāye pathavīdhātu āpodhātu tejodhātu vāyodhātū"ti –
rows.english: [TEXTS AND COMMENTARY IN BRIEF] 28. : “Bhikkhus, just as though a skilled butcher or butcher’s apprentice had killed a cow and were seated at the crossroads [348] with it cut up into pieces, so too, bhikkhus, a bhikkhu reviews this body however placed, however disposed, as consisting of elements: In this body there are the earth element, the water element, the fire element, and the air element” 20 (D II 294).

rows.pali: Evaṃ tikkhapaññassa dhātukammaṭṭhānikassa vasena mahāsatipaṭṭhāne (dī. ni. 2.378) saṅkhepato āgataṃ.
rows.english: It is given in brief in the Mahāsatipaṭṭhāna Sutta, for one of quick understanding whose meditation subject is elements, as (above).

rows.pali: Tassattho – yathā cheko goghātako vā tasseva vā bhattavetanabhato antevāsiko gāviṃ vadhitvā vinivijjhitvā catasso disā gatānaṃ mahāpathānaṃ vemajjhaṭṭhānasaṅkhāte catumahāpathe koṭṭhāsaṃ katvā nisinno assa, evameva bhikkhu catunnaṃ iriyāpathānaṃ yena kenaci ākārena ṭhitattā yathāṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: 29. The meaning is this: just as though a clever butcher, or his apprentice who worked for his keep, had killed a cow and divided it up and were seated at the crossroads, reckoned as the intersection of the main roads going in the four directions, having laid it out part by part, so too a bhikkhu reviews the body, however placed because it is in some one of the four postures

rows.pali: Yathāṭhitattāva yathāpaṇihitaṃ kāyaṃ atthi imasmiṃ kāye pathavīdhātu - pe - vāyodhātūti evaṃ dhātuso paccavekkhati.
rows.english: and however disposed because it is so placed, thus: In this body there are the earth element, the water element, the fire element, and the air element.

rows.pali: Kiṃ vuttaṃ hoti?
rows.english: 30.What is meant?

rows.pali: Yathā goghātakassa gāviṃ posentassapi āghātanaṃ āharantassapi āharitvā tattha bandhitvā ṭhapentassapi vadhantassapi vadhitaṃ mataṃ passantassapi tāvadeva gāvītisaññā na antaradhāyati, yāva naṃ padāletvā bilaso na vibhajati.
rows.english: Just as the butcher, while feeding the cow, bringing it to the shambles, keeping it tied up after bringing it there, slaughtering it, and seeing it slaughtered and dead, does not lose the perception “cow” so long as he has not carved it up and divided it into parts;

rows.pali: Vibhajitvā nisinnassa pana gāvīsaññā antaradhāyati, maṃsasaññā pavattati.
rows.english: but when he has divided it up and is sitting there, he loses the perception “cow” and the perception “meat” occurs;

rows.pali: Nāssa evaṃ hoti "ahaṃ gāviṃ vikkiṇāmi, ime gāviṃ harantī"ti.
rows.english: he does not think “I am selling cow” or “They are carrying cow away,”

rows.pali: Atha khvassa "ahaṃ maṃsaṃ vikkiṇāmi, imepi maṃsaṃ haranti"cceva hoti, evameva imassāpi bhikkhuno pubbe bālaputhujjanakāle gihibhūtassapi pabbajitassapi tāvadeva sattoti vā posoti vā puggaloti vā saññā na antaradhāyati, yāva imameva kāyaṃ yathāṭhitaṃ yathāpaṇihitaṃ ghanavinibbhogaṃ katvā dhātuso na paccavekkhati.
rows.english: but rather he thinks “I am selling meat” or “They are carrying meat away”; so too this bhikkhu, while still a foolish ordinary person—both formerly as a layman and as one gone forth into homelessness—does not lose the perception “living being” or “man” or “person” so long as he does not, by resolution of the compact into elements, review this body, however placed, however disposed, as consisting of elements.

rows.pali: Dhātuso paccavekkhato pana sattasaññā antaradhāyati, dhātuvaseneva cittaṃ santiṭṭhati.
rows.english: But when he does review it as consisting of elements, he loses the perception “living being” and his mind establishes itself upon elements.

rows.pali: Tenāha bhagavā "seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, dakkho goghātako vā - pe - nisinno assa, evameva kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu - pe - vāyodhātū"ti.
rows.english: That is why the Blessed One said: “Bhikkhus, just as though a skilled butcher … were seated at the crossroads … so too, bhikkhus, a bhikkhu … air element. ”

rows.pali: 307.Mahāhatthipadūpame (ma. ni. 1.300 ādayo) pana – "katamā cāvuso, ajjhattikā pathavīdhātu?
rows.english: [IN DETAIL] 31. In the Mahāhatthipadopama Sutta —as follows: “And what is the internal earth element, friends?

rows.pali: Yaṃ ajjhattaṃ paccattaṃ kakkhaḷaṃ kharigataṃ upādinnaṃ.
rows.english: Whatever there is internally in oneself that is hard, harsh,21 and clung to (acquired through kamma),

rows.pali: Seyyathidaṃ, kesā lomā - pe - udariyaṃ karīsaṃ, yaṃ vā panaññampi kiñci ajjhattaṃ paccattaṃ kakkhaḷaṃ kharigataṃ upādinnaṃ, ayaṃ vuccati, āvuso, ajjhattikā pathavīdhātū"ti ca,
rows.english: that is to say, head hairs, body hairs, teeth, nails, skin, flesh, sinews, bones, bone marrow, kidney, heart, liver, midriff, spleen, lungs, bowels, entrails, gorge, dung, or whatever else there is internally in oneself that is hard, harsh, and clung to— this is called the internal earth element” (M I 185). [349]

rows.pali: "Katamā cāvuso, ajjhattikā āpodhātu?
rows.english: And: “What is the internal water element, friends?

rows.pali: Yaṃ ajjhattaṃ paccattaṃ āpo āpogataṃ upādinnaṃ.
rows.english: Whatever there is internally in oneself that is water, watery, and clung to,

rows.pali: Seyyathidaṃ, pittaṃ - pe - muttaṃ, yaṃ vā panaññampi kiñci ajjhattaṃ paccattaṃ āpo āpogataṃ upādinnaṃ, ayaṃ vuccatāvuso, ajjhattikā āpodhātū"ti ca,
rows.english: that is to say, bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat, tears, grease, spittle, snot, oil of the joints, and urine, or whatever else there is internally in oneself that is water, watery, and clung to—this is called the internal water element” (M I 187).

rows.pali: "Katamā cāvuso, ajjhattikā tejodhātu?
rows.english: And: “What is the internal fire element, friends?

rows.pali: Yaṃ ajjhattaṃ paccattaṃ tejo tejogataṃ upādinnaṃ.
rows.english: Whatever there is internally in oneself that is fire, fiery, and clung to,

rows.pali: Seyyathidaṃ, yena ca santappati, yena ca jīrīyati, yena ca pariḍayhati, yena ca asitapītakhāyitasāyitaṃ sammā pariṇāmaṃ gacchati, yaṃ vā panaññampi kiñci ajjhattaṃ paccattaṃ tejo tejogataṃ upādinnaṃ, ayaṃ vuccatāvuso, ajjhattikā tejodhātū"ti ca,
rows.english: that is to say, that whereby one is warmed, ages, and burns up, and whereby what is eaten, drunk, chewed and tasted gets completely digested, or whatever else there is internally in oneself that is fire, fiery, and clung to—this is called the internal fire element” (M I 188).

rows.pali: "Katamā cāvuso, ajjhattikā vāyodhātu?
rows.english: And: “What is the internal air element, friends?

rows.pali: Yaṃ ajjhattaṃ paccattaṃ vāyo vāyogataṃ upādinnaṃ.
rows.english: Whatever there is internally in oneself that is air, airy, and clung to,

rows.pali: Seyyathidaṃ, uddhaṅgamā vātā, adhogamā vātā, kucchisayā vātā, koṭṭhāsayā vātā, aṅgamaṅgānusārino vātā, assāso passāso iti vā, yaṃ vā panaññampi kiñci ajjhattaṃ paccattaṃ vāyo vāyogataṃ upādinnaṃ, ayaṃ vuccatāvuso, ajjhattikā vāyodhātū"ti ca –
rows.english: that is to say, up-going winds, down- going winds, winds in the belly, winds in the bowels, winds that course through all the limbs, in-breath and out-breath, or whatever else there is internally in oneself that is air, airy, and clung to—this is called the internal air element” (M I 188).

rows.pali: Evaṃ nātitikkhapaññassa dhātukammaṭṭhānikassa vasena vitthārato āgataṃ.
rows.english: - it is given in detail for one of not over- quick understanding whose meditation subject is elements—

rows.pali: Yathā cettha, evaṃ rāhulovādadhātuvibhaṅgesupi.
rows.english: and as here so also in the Rāhulovāda and Dhātuvibhaṅga Suttas.

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ anuttānapadavaṇṇanā, ajjhattaṃ paccattanti idaṃ tāva ubhayampi niyakassa adhivacanaṃ.
rows.english: 32.Here is the commentary on the words that are not clear. Internally in oneself (ajjhattaṃ paccattaṃ): both these words are terms for what is one’s own (niyaka), since what is one’s own is what is produced in one’s own self (attani jātaṃ);

rows.pali: Niyakaṃ nāma attani jātaṃ sasantānapariyāpannanti attho.
rows.english: the meaning is, included in one’s continuity (sasantati-pariyāpanna).

rows.pali: Tayidaṃ yathā loke itthīsu kathā adhitthīti vuccati, evaṃ attani pavattattā ajjhattaṃ, attānaṃ paṭicca paṭicca pavattattā paccattantipi vuccati.
rows.english: This is called “internal” (ajjhanaṃ = adhi + attā, lit. “belonging-to-self”) because it occurs in self (attani—locative case) just as in the world, speech among women (itthīsu—loc. case) is called “[speech] belonging-to-women” (adhitthi). And it is called, “in oneself” (paccattaṃ) because it occurs owing to self (attānaṃ paṭicca). 22

rows.pali: Kakkhaḷanti thaddhaṃ.
rows.english: 33.Hard: rigid.

rows.pali: Kharigatanti pharusaṃ.
rows.english: Harsh: rough.

rows.pali: Tattha paṭhamaṃ lakkhaṇavacanaṃ, dutiyaṃ ākāravacanaṃ, kakkhaḷalakkhaṇā hi pathavīdhātu, sā pharusākārā hoti, tasmā kharigatanti vuttā.
rows.english: Herein, the first is a word for the characteristic, while the second is a word for the mode; for the earth element is characterized as hard, but its mode is rough, which is why it is called “harsh.”

rows.pali: Upādinnanti daḷhaṃ ādinnaṃ, ahaṃ mamanti evaṃ daḷhaṃ ādinnaṃ, gahitaṃ parāmaṭṭhanti attho.
rows.english: Clung to: taken firmly [by kamma]; the meaning is, firmly taken, seized, adhered to, as “I,” “mine” (see §89f.).

rows.pali: Seyyathidanti nipāto.
rows.english: 34.That is to say: the word seyyathidaṃ (“that is to say”) is a particle;

rows.pali: Tassa taṃ katamanti ceti attho.
rows.english: its meaning is, “What is that?”

rows.pali: Tato taṃ dassento "kesā lomā"tiādimāha.
rows.english: Next, showing what that is, “head hairs, body hairs,” etc., is said.

rows.pali: Ettha ca matthaluṅgaṃ pakkhipitvā vīsatiyā ākārehi pathavīdhātu niddiṭṭhāti veditabbā.
rows.english: And here the brain must be added since it has to be understood that the earth element needs to be described in twenty modes.

rows.pali: Yaṃ vā panaññampi kiñcīti avasesesu tīsu koṭṭhāsesu pathavīdhātu saṅgahitā.
rows.english: Or whatever else: the earth element included in the remaining three portions.

rows.pali: Vissandanabhāvena taṃ taṃ ṭhānaṃ appotīti āpo.
rows.english: 35.[350] It flows (appoti), flows on (pappoti), to such and such a place as a state of streaming, thus it is water (āpo).

rows.pali: Kammasamuṭṭhānādivasena nānāvidhesu āpesu gatanti āpogataṃ.
rows.english: The watery (āpo-gata) is what is gone (gata) among such various kinds of water (āpo) as the kamma-originated, and so on.

rows.pali: Kiṃ taṃ?
rows.english: What is that?

rows.pali: Āpodhātuyā ābandhanalakkhaṇaṃ.
rows.english: It is what has the water element’s characteristic of cohesion.

rows.pali: Tejanavasena tejo, vuttanayeneva tejesu gatanti tejogataṃ.
rows.english: 36. Fire (tejo) [is definable] as heating (tejana). The fiery (tejo-gata) is what is gone (gata), in the way already described, among the kinds of fire (tejo).

rows.pali: Kiṃ taṃ?
rows.english: What is that?

rows.pali: Uṇhattalakkhaṇaṃ.
rows.english: It is what has the characteristic of heat.

rows.pali: Yena cāti yena tejodhātugatena kupitena ayaṃ kāyo santappati, ekāhikajarādibhāvena usumajāto hoti.
rows.english: Whereby: by means of which the fire element, when excited, this body is warmed, becomes heated by the state of one- day fever,23 and so on.

rows.pali: Yena ca jīrīyatīti yena ayaṃ kāyo jīrati, indriyavekallataṃ balaparikkhayaṃ valipalitādibhāvañca pāpuṇāti.
rows.english: Ages: whereby this body grows old, reaches the decline of the faculties, loss of strength, wrinkles, grayness, and so on.

rows.pali: Yena ca pariḍayhatīti yena kupitena ayaṃ kāyo ḍayhati.
rows.english: Burns up: whereby, when excited, it causes this body to burn,

rows.pali: So ca puggalo "ḍayhāmi ḍayhāmī"ti kandanto satadhotasappigosīsacandanādilepañceva tālavaṇṭavātañca paccāsīsati.
rows.english: and the person cries out, “I am burning, I am burning!” and longs for ghee a hundred times washed and for gosīsa sandalwood ointment, etc., and for the breeze of a fan.

rows.pali: Yena ca asitapītakhāyitasāyitaṃ sammā pariṇāmaṃ gacchatīti yenetaṃ asitaṃ vā odanādi pītaṃ vā pānakādi khāyitaṃ vā piṭṭhakhajjakādi sāyitaṃ vā ambapakkamadhuphāṇitādi sammā paripākaṃ gacchati, rasādibhāvena vivekaṃ gacchatīti attho.
rows.english: And whereby what is eaten, drunk, chewed and tasted gets completely digested: whereby the boiled rice, etc., that is eaten, or the beverage, etc., that is drunk, or the hard food consisting of flour biscuits, etc., that is chewed, the mango fruit, honey, molasses, etc., that is tasted, gets completely cooked; gets its juice, etc., extracted, is the meaning.

rows.pali: Ettha ca purimā tayo tejodhātusamuṭṭhānā.
rows.english: And here the first three kinds of fire element [that is to say, “is warmed,” “ages,” and “burns up”] are of fourfold origination (XX.27ff.),

rows.pali: Pacchimo kammasamuṭṭhānova.
rows.english: while the last is only kamma-originated.

rows.pali: Vāyanavasena vāyo, vuttanayeneva vāyesu gatanti vāyogataṃ.
rows.english: 37.Air (vāyo) [is definable] as blowing (vāyana). The airy (vāyo-gata) is what is gone (gata), in the way already described, among the kinds of air.

rows.pali: Kiṃ taṃ?
rows.english: What is that?

rows.pali: Vitthambhanalakkhaṇaṃ.
rows.english: It is what has the characteristic of distension.24

rows.pali: Uddhaṅgamā vātāti uggārahikkādipavattakā uddhaṃ ārohaṇavātā.
rows.english: Upgoing winds: winds (forces) mounting upwards that cause the occurrence of vomiting, belching, and so on.

rows.pali: Adhogamā vātāti uccārapassāvādinīharaṇakā adho orohaṇavātā.
rows.english: Down-going winds: winds (forces) descending downwards that expel excrement and urine.

rows.pali: Kucchisayā vātāti antānaṃ bahivātā.
rows.english: Winds in the belly: winds (forces) outside the bowels.

rows.pali: Koṭṭhāsayā vātāti antānaṃ antovātā.
rows.english: Winds in the bowels: winds (forces) inside the bowels.

rows.pali: Aṅgamaṅgānusārino vātāti dhamanijālānusārena sakalasarīre aṅgamaṅgāni anusaṭā samiñjanapasāraṇādinibbattakā vātā.
rows.english: Winds that course through all the limbs: winds (forces) that produce flexing, extending, etc., and are distributed over the limbs and the whole body by means of the network of veins (nerves).

rows.pali: Assāsoti antopavisananāsikavāto.
rows.english: In-breath: wind in the nostrils entering in.

rows.pali: Passāsoti bahinikkhamananāsikavāto.
rows.english: Out-breath: wind in the nostrils issuing out.

rows.pali: Ettha ca purimā pañca catusamuṭṭhānā.
rows.english: And here the first five are of fourfold origination.

rows.pali: Assāsapassāsā cittasamuṭṭhānāva.
rows.english: In-breath and out-breath are consciousness-originated. [351]

rows.pali: Sabbattha yaṃ vā panaññampi kiñcīti iminā padena avasesakoṭṭhāsesu āpodhātuādayo saṅgahitā.
rows.english: In each instance the phrase or whatever else comprises respectively the water element, the fire element, or the air element included in the other three portions.

rows.pali: Iti vīsatiyā ākārehi pathavīdhātu, dvādasahi āpodhātu, catūhi tejodhātu, chahi vāyodhātūti dvācattālīsāya ākārehi catasso dhātuyo vitthāritā hontīti ayaṃ tāvettha pāḷivaṇṇanā.
rows.english: 38.So the four elements have been detailed in forty-two aspects, that is to say, the earth element in twenty aspects, the water element in twelve, the fire element in four, and the air element in six. This, firstly, is the commentary on the texts here.

rows.pali: 308.Bhāvanānaye panettha tikkhapaññassa bhikkhuno kesā pathavīdhātu, lomā pathavīdhātūti evaṃ vitthārato dhātupariggaho papañcato upaṭṭhāti.
rows.english: [METHOD OF DEVELOPMENT IN BRIEF] 39.As regards the method of development here, however, to discern the elements in detail in this way, “The head hairs are the earth element, the body hairs are the earth element,” appears redundant to a bhikkhu of quick understanding.

rows.pali: Yaṃ thaddhalakkhaṇaṃ, ayaṃ pathavīdhātu.
rows.english: “What has the characteristic of stiffenedness is the earth element,

rows.pali: Yaṃ ābandhanalakkhaṇaṃ, ayaṃ āpodhātu.
rows.english: what has the characteristic of cohesion is the water element,

rows.pali: Yaṃ paripācanalakkhaṇaṃ, ayaṃ tejodhātu.
rows.english: what has the characteristic of ripening (maturing) is the fire element,

rows.pali: Yaṃ vitthambhanalakkhaṇaṃ, ayaṃ vāyodhātūti evaṃ manasikaroto panassa kammaṭṭhānaṃ pākaṭaṃ hoti.
rows.english: what has the characteristic of distending (supporting) is the air element.” - though the meditation subject becomes clear to him if he gives his attention to it in this way.

rows.pali: Nātitikkhapaññassa pana evaṃ manasikaroto andhakāraṃ avibhūtaṃ hoti.
rows.english: But when one of not over-quick understanding gives his attention to it in this way, it appears obscure and unevident,

rows.pali: Purimanayena vitthārato manasikarontassa pākaṭaṃ hoti.
rows.english: and it only becomes plain to him if he gives his attention to it in the first-mentioned way.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ ?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: Yathā dvīsu bhikkhūsu bahupeyyālaṃ tantiṃ sajjhāyantesu tikkhapañño bhikkhu sakiṃ vā dvikkhattuṃ vā peyyālamukhaṃ vitthāretvā tato paraṃ ubhatokoṭivaseneva sajjhāyaṃ karonto gacchati.
rows.english: 40.Suppose two bhikkhus are reciting a text with many elided repetitions, then the bhikkhu with the quicker understanding fills out the elided repetitions once or twice, after which he goes on doing the recital with only the two end parts of the elisions.

rows.pali: Tatra nātitikkhapañño evaṃ vattā hoti "kiṃ sajjhāyo nāmesa oṭṭhapariyāhatamattaṃ kātuṃ na deti, evaṃ sajjhāye kariyamāne kadā tanti paguṇā bhavissatī"ti.
rows.english: Here the one of less quick understanding says, “What is he reciting? Why, he does not even give one time to move one’s lips! If the recitation is done like this, when shall we ever get familiar with the text?”

rows.pali: So āgatāgataṃ peyyālamukhaṃ vitthāretvāva sajjhāyaṃ karoti.
rows.english: and so he does his recitation filling out each elision as it comes.

rows.pali: Tamenaṃ itaro evamāha – "kiṃ sajjhāyo nāmesa pariyosānaṃ gantuṃ na deti, evaṃ sajjhāye kariyamāne kadā tanti pariyosānaṃ gamissatī"ti.
rows.english: Then the other says, “What is he reciting? Why, he never lets one get to the end of it! If the recitation is done like this; when shall we ever get to the end of it?”

rows.pali: Evameva tikkhapaññassa kesādivasena vitthārato dhātupariggaho papañcato upaṭṭhāti.
rows.english: So too, the detailed discerning of the elements by head hairs, etc., appears redundant to one of quick understanding,

rows.pali: Yaṃ thaddhalakkhaṇaṃ, ayaṃ pathavīdhātūtiādinā nayena saṅkhepato manasikaroto kammaṭṭhānaṃ pākaṭaṃ hoti.
rows.english: though the meditation subject becomes clear to him if he gives his attention to it in brief in this way, “What has the characteristic of stiffenedness is the earth element,” and so on.

rows.pali: Itarassa tathā manasikaroto andhakāraṃ avibhūtaṃ hoti.
rows.english: But when the other gives his attention to it in this way, it appears obscure and unevident,

rows.pali: Kesādivasena vitthārato manasikarontassa pākaṭaṃ hoti.
rows.english: and it only becomes plain to him if he gives his attention in detail by head hairs and so on.

rows.pali: Tasmā imaṃ kammaṭṭhānaṃ bhāvetukāmena tikkhapaññena tāva rahogatena paṭisallīnena sakalampi attano rūpakāyaṃ āvajjetvā yo imasmiṃ kāye thaddhabhāvo vā kharabhāvo vā, ayaṃ pathavīdhātu.
rows.english: 41.So firstly, one of quick understanding who wants to develop this meditation subject should go into solitary retreat. Then he should advert to his own entire material body and discern the elements in brief in this way: “In this body what is stiffenedness or harshness is the earth element,

rows.pali: Yo ābandhanabhāvo vā dravabhāvo vā, ayaṃ āpodhātu.
rows.english: what is cohesion or fluidity25 [352] is the water element,

rows.pali: Yo paripācanabhāvo vā uṇhabhāvo vā, ayaṃ tejodhātu.
rows.english: what is maturing (ripening) or heat is the fire element,

rows.pali: Yo vitthambhanabhāvo vā samudīraṇabhāvo vā, ayaṃ vāyodhātūti evaṃ saṃkhittena dhātuyo pariggahetvā punappunaṃ pathavīdhātu āpodhātūti dhātumattato nissattato nijjīvato āvajjitabbaṃ manasikātabbaṃ paccavekkhitabbaṃ.
rows.english: what is distension or movement is the air element.” And he should advert and give attention to it and review it again and again as “earth element, water element,” that is to say, as mere elements, not a living being, and soulless.

rows.pali: Tassevaṃ vāyamamānassa nacireneva dhātuppabhedāvabhāsanapaññāpariggahito sabhāvadhammārammaṇattā appanaṃ appatto upacāramatto samādhi uppajjati.
rows.english: 42.As he makes effort in this way it is not long before concentration arises in him, which is reinforced by understanding that illuminates the classification of the elements, and which is only access and does not reach absorption because it has states with individual essences as its object.

rows.pali: Atha vā pana ye ime catunnaṃ mahābhūtānaṃ nissattabhāvadassanatthaṃ dhammasenāpatinā "aṭṭhiñca paṭicca nhāruñca paṭicca maṃsañca paṭicca cammañca paṭicca ākāso parivārito rūpantveva saṅkhaṃ gacchatī"ti (ma. ni. 1.306) cattāro koṭṭhāsā vuttā.
rows.english: 43.Or alternatively, there are these four [bodily] parts mentioned by the General of the Dhamma [the Elder Sāriputta] for the purpose of showing the absence of any living being in the four great primary elements thus: “When a space is enclosed with bones and sinews and flesh and skin, there comes to be the term ‘material form’ (rūpa)” (M I 190).

rows.pali: Tesu taṃ taṃ antarānusārinā ñāṇahatthena vinibbhujitvā vinibbhujitvā yo etesu thaddhabhāvo vā kharabhāvo vā, ayaṃ pathavīdhātūti purimanayeneva dhātuyo pariggahetvā punappunaṃ pathavīdhātu āpodhātūti dhātumattato nissattato nijjīvato āvajjitabbaṃ manasikātabbaṃ paccavekkhitabbaṃ.
rows.english: And he should resolve each of these [as a separate entity], separating them out by the hand of knowledge, and then discern them in the way already stated thus: “In these what is stiffenedness or harshness is the earth element. ” And he should again and again advert to them, give attention to them and review them as mere elements, not a living being, not a soul.

rows.pali: Tassevaṃ vāyamamānassa nacireneva dhātuppabhedāvabhāsanapaññāpariggahito sabhāvadhammārammaṇattā appanaṃ appatto upacāramatto samādhi uppajjati.
rows.english: 44.As he makes effort in this way, it is not long before concentration arises in him, which is reinforced by understanding that illuminates the classification of the elements, and which is only access and does not reach absorption because it has states with individual essences as its object.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ saṅkhepato āgate catudhātuvavatthāne bhāvanānayo.
rows.english: This is the method of development when the definition of the elements is given in brief.

rows.pali: 309.Vitthārato āgate pana evaṃ veditabbo.
rows.english: [METHOD OF DEVELOPMENT IN DETAIL] 45.The method given in detail should be understood in this way.

rows.pali: Idaṃ kammaṭṭhānaṃ bhāvetukāmena hi nātitikkhapaññena yoginā ācariyasantike dvācattālīsāya ākārehi vitthārato dhātuyo uggaṇhitvā vuttappakāre senāsane viharantena katasabbakiccena rahogatena paṭisallīnena sasambhārasaṅkhepato, sasambhāravibhattito, salakkhaṇasaṅkhepato, salakkhaṇavibhattitoti evaṃ catūhākārehi kammaṭṭhānaṃ bhāvetabbaṃ.
rows.english: A meditator of not over-quick understanding who wants to develop this meditation subject should learn the elements in detail in the forty-two aspects from a teacher, and he should live in an abode of the kind already described. Then, when he has done all the duties, he should go into solitary retreat and develop the meditation subject in four ways thus: (1) with constituents in brief, (2) with constituents by analysis, (3) with characteristics in brief, and (4) with characteristics by analysis.

rows.pali: Tattha kathaṃ sasambhārasaṅkhepato bhāveti?
rows.english: [(1) WITH CONSTITUENTS IN BRIEF] 46.Herein, how does he develop it with constituents in brief?

rows.pali: Idha bhikkhu vīsatiyā koṭṭhāsesu thaddhākāraṃ pathavīdhātūti vavatthapeti.
rows.english: Here a bhikkhu does his defining in this way, “In twenty of the parts what has the stiffened mode is the earth element,”

rows.pali: Dvādasasu koṭṭhāsesu yūsagataṃ udakasaṅkhātaṃ ābandhanākāraṃ āpodhātūti vavatthapeti.
rows.english: and he does his defining thus, “In twelve parts the liquid called water with the mode of cohesion is the water element,”

rows.pali: Catūsu koṭṭhāsesu paripācanakaṃ tejaṃ tejodhātūti vavatthapeti.
rows.english: [353] and he does his defining thus, “In four parts what matures (what has the mode of ripening) is the fire element,”

rows.pali: Chasu koṭṭhāsesu vitthambhanākāraṃ vāyodhātūti vavatthapeti.
rows.english: and he does his defining thus, “In six parts what has the mode of distending is the air element.”

rows.pali: Tassevaṃ vavatthāpayatoyeva dhātuyo pākaṭā honti.
rows.english: As he defines them in this way they become evident to him.

rows.pali: Tā punappunaṃ āvajjato manasikaroto vuttanayeneva upacārasamādhi uppajjati.
rows.english: As he again and again adverts to them and gives his attention to them, concentration arises as access only.

rows.pali: 310.Yassa pana evaṃ bhāvayato kammaṭṭhānaṃ na ijjhati, tena sasambhāravibhattito bhāvetabbaṃ.
rows.english: [(2) WITH CONSTITUENTS BY ANALYSIS] 47.However, if his meditation subject is not successful while he develops it in this way, then he should develop it with constituents by analysis.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Tena hi bhikkhunā yaṃ taṃ kāyagatāsatikammaṭṭhānaniddese sattadhā uggahakosallaṃ dasadhā manasikārakosallañca vuttaṃ.
rows.english: Firstly, the bhikkhu should carry out all the directions given for the thirty-two-fold aspect in the description of mindfulness occupied with the body as a meditation subject (VIII.48–78), namely, the sevenfold skill in learning and the tenfold skill in giving attention,

rows.pali: Dvattiṃsākāre tāva taṃ sabbaṃ aparihāpetvā tacapañcakādīnaṃ anulomapaṭilomato vacasā sajjhāyaṃ ādiṃkatvā sabbaṃ tattha vuttavidhānaṃ kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: and he should start with the verbal recitation, in direct and reverse order, of the skin pentad and so on, without omitting any of it.

rows.pali: Ayameva hi viseso, tattha vaṇṇasaṇṭhānadisokāsaparicchedavasena kesādayo manasikaritvāpi paṭikkūlavasena cittaṃ ṭhapetabbaṃ, idha dhātuvasena.
rows.english: The only difference is this: there, after giving attention to the head hairs, etc., as to colour, shape, direction, location, and delimitation, the mind had to be fixed by means of repulsiveness (VIII.83), but here it is done by means of elements.

rows.pali: Tasmā vaṇṇādivasena pañcadhā pañcadhā kesādayo manasikaritvā avasāne evaṃ manasikāro pavattetabbo.
rows.english: Therefore at the end of each part after giving attention to head hairs, etc., each in the five ways beginning with colour (VIII.83), attention should be given as follows.

rows.pali: 311.Ime kesā nāma sīsakaṭāhapaliveṭhanacamme jātā.
rows.english: 48. These things called head hairs grow on the inner skin that envelops the skull.

rows.pali: Tattha yathāvammikamatthake jātesu kuṇṭhatiṇesu na vammikamatthako jānāti mayi kuṇṭhatiṇāni jātānīti, napi kuṇṭhatiṇāni jānanti mayaṃ vammikamatthake jātānīti, evameva na sīsakaṭāhapaliveṭhanacammaṃ jānāti mayi kesā jātāti, napi kesā jānanti mayaṃ sīsakaṭāhaveṭhanacamme jātāti, aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: Herein, just as when kuṇṭha grasses grow on the top of an anthill, the top of the termite-mound does not know, “Kuṇṭha grasses are growing on me,” nor do the kuṇṭha grasses know, “We are growing on the top of a termite-mound,” so too, the inner skin that covers the skull does not know, “Head hairs grow on me,” nor do the head hairs know, “We grow on inner skin that envelops a skull. ” These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti kesā nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto thaddho pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: So what are called head hairs are a particular component of this body, without thought, [morally] indeterminate, void, not a living being, rigid (stiffened) earth element.

rows.pali: 312.Lomā sarīraveṭhanacamme jātā.
rows.english: 49.Body hairs grow on the inner skin that envelops the body.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā suññagāmaṭṭhāne jātesu dabbatiṇakesu na suññagāmaṭṭhānaṃ jānāti mayi dabbatiṇakāni jātānīti, napi dabbatiṇakāni jānanti mayaṃ suññagāmaṭṭhāne jātānīti, evameva na sarīraveṭhanacammaṃ jānāti mayi lomā jātāti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when dabba grasses grow on the square in an empty village, the square in the empty village does not know, “Dabba grasses grow on me,” nor do the dabba grasses know, “We grow on the square in an empty village,” so too, the inner skin that envelops the body does not know, “Body hairs grow on me,”

rows.pali: Napi lomā jānanti mayaṃ sarīraveṭhanacamme jātāti.
rows.english: nor do the body hairs know, “We grow on inner skin that envelops a body.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti lomā nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto thaddho pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: So what are called body hairs are a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, rigid earth element.

rows.pali: 313.Nakhā aṅgulīnaṃ aggesu jātā.
rows.english: 50.Nails grow on the tips of the fingers and toes.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā kumārakesu daṇḍakehi madhukaṭṭhike vijjhitvā kīḷantesu na daṇḍakā jānanti amhesu madhukaṭṭhikā ṭhapitāti, napi madhukaṭṭhikā jānanti mayaṃ daṇḍakesu ṭhapitāti, evameva na aṅguliyo jānanti amhākaṃ aggesu nakhā jātāti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when children play a game by piercing madhuka-fruit kernels with sticks, the sticks [354] do not know, “Madhuka-fruit kernels are put on us,” nor do the madhuka-fruit kernels know, “We are put on sticks,” so too, the fingers and toes do not know, “Nails grow on our tips,”

rows.pali: Napi nakhā jānanti mayaṃ aṅgulīnaṃ aggesu jātāti.
rows.english: nor do the nails know, “We grow on the tips of fingers and toes.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti nakhā nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto thaddho pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: So what are called nails are a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, rigid earth element.

rows.pali: 314.Dantā hanukaṭṭhikesu jātā.
rows.english: 51. Teeth grow in the jaw bones.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā vaḍḍhakīhi pāsāṇaudukkhalakesu kenacideva silesajātena bandhitvā ṭhapitathambhesu na udukkhalā jānanti amhesu thambhā ṭhitāti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when posts are placed by builders in stone sockets and fastened with some kind of cement,26 the sockets do not know, “Posts are placed in us,”

rows.pali: Napi thambhā jānanti mayaṃ udukkhalesu ṭhitāti, evameva na hanukaṭṭhīni jānanti amhesu dantā jātāti.
rows.english: nor do the posts know, “We are placed in sockets,” so too, the jaw bones do not know, “Teeth grow in us,”

rows.pali: Napi dantā jānanti mayaṃ hanukaṭṭhīsu jātāti.
rows.english: nor do the teeth know, “We grow in jaw bones’.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti dantā nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto thaddho pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: So what are called teeth are a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, rigid earth element.

rows.pali: 315.Taco sakalasarīraṃ pariyonandhitvā ṭhito.
rows.english: 52.Skin is to be found covering the whole body.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā allagocammapariyonaddhāya mahāvīṇāya na mahāvīṇā jānāti ahaṃ allagocammena pariyonaddhāti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when a big lute is covered with damp ox-hide, the lute does not know, “I am covered with damp ox-hide,”

rows.pali: Napi allagocammaṃ jānāti mayā mahāvīṇā pariyonaddhāti, evameva na sarīraṃ jānāti ahaṃ tacena pariyonaddhanti.
rows.english: nor does the damp ox-hide know, “A lute is covered by me,” so too, the body does not know, “I am covered by skin,”

rows.pali: Napi taco jānāti mayā sarīraṃ pariyonaddhanti.
rows.english: nor does the skin know, “A body is covered by me.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti taco nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto thaddho pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called skin is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, rigid earth element.

rows.pali: 316.Maṃsaṃ aṭṭhisaṅghāṭaṃ anulimpitvā ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: 53.Flesh is to be found plastered over the framework of bones.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā mahāmattikalittāya bhittiyā na bhitti jānāti ahaṃ mahāmattikāya littāti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when a wall is plastered with thick clay, the wall does not know, “I am plastered with thick clay,”

rows.pali: Napi mahāmattikā jānāti mayā bhitti littāti, evameva na aṭṭhisaṅghāṭo jānāti ahaṃ navapesisatappabhedena maṃsena littoti.
rows.english: nor does the thick clay know, “A wall is plastered with me,” so too, the framework of bones does not know, “I am plastered with flesh consisting of nine hundred pieces of flesh,”

rows.pali: Napi maṃsaṃ jānāti mayā aṭṭhisaṅghāṭo littoti.
rows.english: nor does the flesh know, “A framework of bones is plastered with me.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti maṃsaṃ nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto thaddho pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called flesh is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, rigid earth element.

rows.pali: 317.Nhāru sarīrabbhantare aṭṭhīni ābandhamānā ṭhitā.
rows.english: 54.Sinews are to be found in the interior of the body binding the bones together.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā vallīhi vinaddhesu kuṭṭadārūsu na kuṭṭadārūni jānanti mayaṃ vallīhi vinaddhānīti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when withies and sticks are bound together with creepers, the withies and sticks do not know [355] “We are bound together with creepers,”

rows.pali: Napi valliyo jānanti amhehi kuṭṭadārūni vinaddhānīti, evameva na aṭṭhīni jānanti mayaṃ nhārūhi ābaddhānīti.
rows.english: nor do the creepers know, “Withies and sticks are bound together by us,” so too, the bones do not know, “We are bound by sinews,”

rows.pali: Napi nhārū jānanti amhehi aṭṭhīni ābaddhānīti.
rows.english: nor do the sinews know, “Bones are bound together by us.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti nhāru nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto thaddho pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: So what are called sinews are a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, rigid earth element.

rows.pali: 318.Aṭṭhīsu paṇhikaṭṭhi gopphakaṭṭhiṃ ukkhipitvā ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: 55.As to the bones, the heel bone is to be found holding up the ankle bone,

rows.pali: Gopphakaṭṭhi jaṅghaṭṭhiṃ ukkhipitvā ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: the ankle bone holding up the shin bone,

rows.pali: Jaṅghaṭṭhi ūruṭṭhiṃ ukkhipitvā ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: the shin bone the thigh bone,

rows.pali: Ūruṭṭhi kaṭiṭṭhiṃ ukkhipitvā ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: the thigh bone the hip bone,

rows.pali: Kaṭiṭṭhi piṭṭhikaṇṭakaṃ ukkhipitvā ṭhitaṃ, piṭṭhikaṇṭako gīvaṭṭhiṃ ukkhipitvā ṭhito.
rows.english: the hip bone the backbone, the backbone the neck bone,

rows.pali: Gīvaṭṭhi sīsaṭṭhiṃ ukkhipitvā ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: and the neck bone is to be found holding up the cranium bone.

rows.pali: Sīsaṭṭhi gīvaṭṭhike patiṭṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: The cranium bone rests on the neck bone,

rows.pali: Gīvaṭṭhi piṭṭhikaṇṭake patiṭṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: the neck bone on the backbone,

rows.pali: Piṭṭhikaṇṭako kaṭiṭṭhimhi patiṭṭhito.
rows.english: the backbone on the hip bone,

rows.pali: Kaṭiṭṭhi ūruṭṭhike patiṭṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: the hip bone on the thigh bone,

rows.pali: Ūruṭṭhi jaṅghaṭṭhike patiṭṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: the thigh bone on the shin bone,

rows.pali: Jaṅghaṭṭhi gopphakaṭṭhike patiṭṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: the shin bone on the ankle bone,

rows.pali: Gopphakaṭṭhi paṇhikaṭṭhike patiṭṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: the ankle bone on the heel bone.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā iṭṭhakadārugomayādisañcayesu na heṭṭhimā heṭṭhimā jānanti mayaṃ uparime uparime ukkhipitvā ṭhitāti.
rows.english: 56.Herein, just as, when bricks, timber or [blocks of dried] cow dung are built up, those below do not know, “We each stand holding up those above us,”

rows.pali: Napi uparimā uparimā jānanti mayaṃ heṭṭhimesu heṭṭhimesu patiṭṭhitāti, evameva na paṇhikaṭṭhi jānāti ahaṃ gopphakaṭṭhiṃ ukkhipitvā ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor do those above know, “We each rest on those below us,” so too, the heel bone does not know, “I stand holding up the ankle bone,”

rows.pali: Na gopphakaṭṭhi jānāti ahaṃ jaṅghaṭṭhiṃ ukkhipitvā ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the ankle bone know, “I stand holding up the shin bone,”

rows.pali: Na jaṅghaṭṭhi jānāti ahaṃ ūruṭṭhiṃ ukkhipitvā ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the shin bone know, “I stand holding up the thigh bone,”

rows.pali: Na ūruṭṭhi jānāti ahaṃ kaṭiṭṭhiṃ ukkhipitvā ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the thigh bone know, “I stand holding up the hip bone,”

rows.pali: Na kaṭiṭṭhi jānāti ahaṃ piṭṭhikaṇṭakaṃ ukkhipitvā ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the hip bone know, “I stand holding up the backbone,”

rows.pali: Na piṭṭhikaṇṭako jānāti ahaṃ gīvaṭṭhiṃ ukkhipitvā ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the backbone know, “I stand holding up the neck bone,”

rows.pali: Na gīvaṭṭhi jānāti ahaṃ sīsaṭṭhiṃ ukkhipitvā ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the neck bone know, “I stand holding up the cranium bone,”

rows.pali: Na sīsaṭṭhi jānāti ahaṃ gīvaṭṭhimhi patiṭṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the cranium bone know, “I rest on the neck bone,”

rows.pali: Na gīvaṭṭhi jānāti ahaṃ piṭṭhikaṇṭake patiṭṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the neck bone know, “I rest on the backbone,”

rows.pali: Na piṭṭhikaṇṭako jānāti ahaṃ kaṭiṭṭhimhi patiṭṭhitoti.
rows.english: nor does the backbone know, “I rest on the hip bone,”

rows.pali: Na kaṭiṭṭhi jānāti ahaṃ ūruṭṭhimhi patiṭṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the hip bone know, “I rest on the thigh bone,”

rows.pali: Na ūruṭṭhi jānāti ahaṃ jaṅghaṭṭhimhi patiṭṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the thigh bone know, “I rest on the shin bone,”

rows.pali: Na jaṅghaṭṭhi jānāti ahaṃ gopphakaṭṭhimhi patiṭṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the shin bone know, “I rest on the ankle bone,”

rows.pali: Na gopphakaṭṭhi jānāti ahaṃ paṇhikaṭṭhimhi patiṭṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the ankle bone know, “I rest on the heel bone.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti aṭṭhi nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto thaddho pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: So what are called bones [356] are a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, rigid earth element.

rows.pali: 319.Aṭṭhimiñjaṃ tesaṃ tesaṃ aṭṭhīnaṃ abbhantare ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: 57.Bone marrow is to be found inside the various bones.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā veḷupabbādīnaṃ anto pakkhittachinnavettaggādīsu na veḷupabbādīni jānanti amhesu vettaggādīni pakkhittānīti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when boiled bamboo sprouts, etc., are put inside bamboo joints, etc., the bamboo joints, etc., do not know, “Bamboo sprouts, etc., are put in us,”

rows.pali: Napi vettaggādīni jānanti mayaṃ veḷupabbādīsu ṭhitānīti, evameva na aṭṭhīni jānanti amhākaṃ anto miñjaṃ ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor do the bamboo sprouts, etc., know, “We are inside bamboo joints, etc.,” so too, the bones do not know, “Marrow is inside us,”

rows.pali: Nāpi miñjaṃ jānāti ahaṃ aṭṭhīnaṃ anto ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the bone marrow know, “I am inside bones.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti aṭṭhimiñjaṃ nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto thaddho pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called bone marrow is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, rigid earth element.

rows.pali: 320.Vakkaṃ galavāṭakato nikkhantena ekamūlena thokaṃ gantvā dvidhā bhinnena thūlanhārunā vinibaddhaṃ hutvā hadayamaṃsaṃ parikkhipitvā ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: 58.Kidney is to be found on each side of the heart flesh, being fastened by the stout sinew that starts out with a single root from the base of the neck and divides into two after going a short way.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā vaṇṭupanibaddhe ambaphaladvaye na vaṇṭaṃ jānāti mayā ambaphaladvayaṃ upanibaddhanti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when a pair of mango fruits are bound together by their stalk, the stalk does not know, “A pair of mango fruits is bound together by me,”

rows.pali: Napi ambaphaladvayaṃ jānāti ahaṃ vaṇṭena upanibaddhanti, evameva na thūlanhāru jānāti mayā vakkaṃ upanibaddhanti.
rows.english: nor do the pair of mango fruits know, “We are bound together by a stalk,” so too, the stout sinew does not know, “Kidneys are bound together by me,”

rows.pali: Napi vakkaṃ jānāti ahaṃ thūlanhārunā upanibaddhanti.
rows.english: nor does the kidney know, “I am bound together by a stout sinew.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti vakkaṃ nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto thaddho pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called kidney is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, rigid earth element.

rows.pali: 321.Hadayaṃ sarīrabbhantare uraṭṭhipañjaramajjhaṃ nissāya ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: 59.Heart is to be found in the inside of the body near the middle of the frame of the ribs.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā jiṇṇasandamānikapañjaraṃ nissāya ṭhapitāya maṃsapesiyā na sandamānikapañjarabbhantaraṃ jānāti maṃ nissāya maṃsapesi ṭhitāti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when a piece of meat is placed near the framework of an old cart, the inside of the framework of the old cart does not know, “A piece of meat is placed near the middle of me,”

rows.pali: Napi maṃsapesi jānāti ahaṃ jiṇṇasandamānikapañjaraṃ nissāya ṭhitāti, evameva na uraṭṭhipañjarabbhantaraṃ jānāti maṃ nissāya hadayaṃ ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the piece of meat know, “I am near the middle of the inside of the framework of an old cart,” so too, the inside of the framework of the ribs does not know, “A heart is near the middle of me,”

rows.pali: Napi hadayaṃ jānāti ahaṃ uraṭṭhipañjaraṃ nissāya ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the heart know, “I am near the middle of the inside of a framework of ribs.

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: ” These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti hadayaṃ nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto thaddho pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called heart is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, rigid earth element.

rows.pali: 322.Yakanaṃ antosarīre dvinnaṃ thanānamabbhantare dakkhiṇapassaṃ nissāya ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: 60. Liver is to be found inside the body, near the right side between the two breasts.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā ukkhalikapālapassamhi lagge yamakamaṃsapiṇḍe na ukkhalikapālapassaṃ jānāti mayi yamakamaṃsapiṇḍo laggoti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when a twin lump of meat is stuck on the side of a cooking pot, the side of the cooking pot does not know, “A twin lump of meat is stuck on me,”

rows.pali: Napi yamakamaṃsapiṇḍo jānāti ahaṃ ukkhalikapālapasse laggoti, evameva na thanānamabbhantare dakkhiṇapassaṃ jānāti maṃ nissāya yakanaṃ ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the twin lump of meat know, [357] “I am stuck on the side of a cooking pot,” so too, the right side between the breasts does not know, “Liver is near me,”

rows.pali: Napi yakanaṃ jānāti ahaṃ thanānamabbhantare dakkhiṇapassaṃ nissāya ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the liver know, “I am near a right side between two breasts.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti yakanaṃ nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto thaddho pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called liver is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, rigid earth element.

rows.pali: 323.Kilomakesu paṭicchannakilomakaṃ hadayañca vakkañca parivāretvā ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: 61.As to the midriff, the concealed midriff is to be found surrounding the heart and kidney,

rows.pali: Appaṭicchannakilomakaṃ sakalasarīre cammassa heṭṭhato maṃsaṃ pariyonandhitvā ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: while the unconcealed midriff is to be found covering the flesh under the skin in the whole body.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā pilotikapaliveṭhite maṃse na maṃsaṃ jānāti ahaṃ pilotikāya paliveṭhitanti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when meat is wrapped in a rag, the meat does not know, “I am wrapped in a rag,”

rows.pali: Napi pilotikā jānāti mayā maṃsaṃ paliveṭhitanti, evameva na vakkahadayāni sakalasarīre ca maṃsaṃ jānāti ahaṃ kilomakena paṭicchannanti.
rows.english: nor does the rag know, “Meat is wrapped in me,” so too, the heart and kidney, and the flesh in the whole body, do not know, “I am concealed by midriff,”

rows.pali: Napi kilomakaṃ jānāti mayā vakkahadayāni sakalasarīre ca maṃsaṃ paṭicchannanti.
rows.english: nor does the midriff know, “Heart and kidney, and flesh in a whole body, are concealed by me.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti kilomakaṃ nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto thaddho pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called midriff is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, rigid earth element.

rows.pali: 324.Pihakaṃ hadayassa vāmapasse udarapaṭalassa matthakapassaṃ nissāya ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: 62.Spleen is to be found near the upper side of the belly lining on the left side of the heart.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā koṭṭhamatthakapassaṃ nissāya ṭhitāya gomayapiṇḍiyā na koṭṭhamatthakapassaṃ jānāti gomayapiṇḍi maṃ nissāya ṭhitāti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when a lump of cow dung is near the upper side of a barn, the upper side of the barn does not know, “A lump of cow dung is near me,”

rows.pali: Napi gomayapiṇḍi jānāti ahaṃ koṭṭhamatthakapassaṃ nissāya ṭhitāti, evameva na udarapaṭalassa matthakapassaṃ jānāti pihakaṃ maṃ nissāya ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the lump of cow dung know, “I am near the upper side of a barn,” so too, the upper side of the belly lining does not know, “Spleen is near me,”

rows.pali: Napi pihakaṃ jānāti ahaṃ udarapaṭalassa matthakapassaṃ nissāya ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the spleen know, “I am near the upper side of a belly lining.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti pihakaṃ nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto thaddho pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called spleen is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, rigid earth element.

rows.pali: 325.Papphāsaṃsarīrabbhantare dvinnaṃ thanānamantare hadayañca yakanañca upari chādetvā olambantaṃ ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: 63.Lungs are to be found inside the body between the two breasts, hanging over the heart and liver and concealing them.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā jiṇṇakoṭṭhabbhantare lambamāne sakuṇakulāvake na jiṇṇakoṭṭhabbhantaraṃ jānāti mayi sakuṇakulāvako lambamāno ṭhitoti.
rows.english: Herein, just as when a bird’s nest is hanging inside an old barn, the inside of the old barn does not know, “A bird’s nest is hanging in me,”
