rows.pali: Napi sakuṇakulāvako jānāti ahaṃ jiṇṇakoṭṭhabbhantare lambamāno ṭhitoti, evameva na taṃ sarīrabbhantaraṃ jānāti mayi papphāsaṃ lambamānaṃ ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the bird’s nest know, “I am hanging inside an old barn,” so too, [358] the inside of the body does not know, “Lungs are hanging in me,”

rows.pali: Napi papphāsaṃ jānāti ahaṃ evarūpe sarīrabbhantare lambamānaṃ ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor do the lungs know, “We are hanging inside such a body.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti papphāsaṃ nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto thaddho pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called lungs is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, rigid earth element.

rows.pali: 326.Antaṃ galavāṭakakarīsamaggapariyante sarīrabbhantare ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: 64.Bowel is to be found inside the body extending from the base of the neck to the excrement passage.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā lohitadoṇikāya obhujitvā ṭhapite chinnasīsadhammanikaḷevare na lohitadoṇi jānāti mayi dhammanikaḷevaraṃ ṭhitanti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when the carcass of a large beheaded rat snake27 is coiled up and put into a trough of blood, the red trough does not know, “A rat snake’s carcass has been put in me,”

rows.pali: Napi dhammanikaḷevaraṃ jānāti ahaṃ lohitadoṇiyā ṭhitanti, evameva na sarīrabbhantaraṃ jānāti mayi antaṃ ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the rat snake’s carcass know, “I am in a red trough,” so too, the inside of the body does not know, “A bowel is in me,”

rows.pali: Napi antaṃ jānāti ahaṃ sarīrabbhantare ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the bowel know, “I am in a body.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti antaṃ nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto thaddho pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called the bowel is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, rigid earth element.

rows.pali: 327.Antaguṇaṃ antantare ekavīsatiantabhoge bandhitvā ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: 65.Entrails are to be found in the interspaces between the twenty-one coils of the bowel, binding them together.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā pādapuñchanarajjumaṇḍalakaṃ sibbetvā ṭhitesu rajjukesu na pādapuñchanarajjumaṇḍalakaṃ jānāti rajjukā maṃ sibbitvā ṭhitāti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when ropes are found sewing together a rope ring for wiping the feet, the rope ring for wiping the feet does not know, “Ropes are to be found sewing me together,”

rows.pali: Napi rajjukā jānanti mayaṃ pādapuñchanarajjumaṇḍalakaṃ sibbitvā ṭhitāti, evameva na antaṃ jānāti antaguṇaṃ maṃ ābandhitvā ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor do the ropes know, “We are to be found sewing together a rope ring,” so too, the bowel does not know, “Entrails are to be found binding me together,”

rows.pali: Napi antaguṇaṃ jānāti ahaṃ antaṃ ābandhitvā ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor do the entrails know, “We are to be found binding a bowel together.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti antaguṇaṃ nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto thaddho pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called entrails is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, rigid earth element.

rows.pali: 328.Udariyaṃ udare ṭhitaṃ asitapītakhāyitasāyitaṃ.
rows.english: 66.Gorge is what is eaten, drunk, chewed and tasted that lies in the stomach.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā suvānadoṇiyaṃ ṭhite suvānavamathumhi na suvānadoṇi jānāti mayi suvānavamathu ṭhitoti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when a dog’s vomit lies in a dog’s bowl, the dog’s bowl does not know, “Dog’s vomit is lying in me,”

rows.pali: Napi suvānavamathu jānāti ahaṃ suvānadoṇiyaṃ ṭhitoti, evameva na udaraṃ jānāti mayi udariyaṃ ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the dog’s vomit know, “I am lying in a dog’s bowl,” so too, the stomach does not know, “Gorge is lying in me,”

rows.pali: Napi udariyaṃ jānāti ahaṃ udare ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the gorge know, “I am lying in a stomach.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti udariyaṃ nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto thaddho pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called gorge is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, rigid earth element.

rows.pali: 329.Karīsaṃ pakkāsayasaṅkhāte aṭṭhaṅgulaveḷupabbasadise antapariyosāne ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: 67.Dung is to be found at the end of the bowel, which resembles a bamboo joint eight fingerbreadths long and is called the “receptacle for digested food.” [359]

rows.pali: Tattha yathā veḷupabbe omadditvā pakkhittāya saṇhapaṇḍumattikāya na veḷupabbaṃ jānāti mayi paṇḍumattikā ṭhitāti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when soft brown clay is impacted in a bamboo joint, the bamboo joint does not know, “Brown clay is in me,”

rows.pali: Napi paṇḍumattikā jānāti ahaṃ veḷupabbe ṭhitāti, evameva na pakkāsayo jānāti mayi karīsaṃ ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does brown clay know, “I am in a bamboo joint,” so too, the receptacle for digested food does not know, “Dung is in me,”

rows.pali: Napi karīsaṃ jānāti ahaṃ pakkāsaye ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the dung know, “I am in a receptacle for digested food.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti karīsaṃ nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto thaddho pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called dung is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, rigid earth element.

rows.pali: 330.Matthaluṅgaṃ sīsakaṭāhabbhantare ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: 68.Brain is to be found in the interior of the skull.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā purāṇalābukaṭāhe pakkhittāya piṭṭhapiṇḍiyā na lābukaṭāhaṃ jānāti mayi piṭṭhapiṇḍi ṭhitāti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when a lump of dough is put inside an old gourd rind, the gourd rind does not know, “A lump of dough is in me,”

rows.pali: Napi piṭṭhapiṇḍi jānāti ahaṃ lābukaṭāhe ṭhitāti, evameva na sīsakaṭāhabbhantaraṃ jānāti mayi matthaluṅgaṃ ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the lump of dough know, “I am inside a gourd rind,” so too, the inside of the skull does not know, “Brain is in me,”

rows.pali: Napi matthaluṅgaṃ jānāti ahaṃ sīsakaṭāhabbhantare ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the brain know, “I am inside a skull.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti matthaluṅgaṃ nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto thaddho pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called brain is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, rigid earth element.

rows.pali: 331.Pittesu abaddhapittaṃ jīvitindriyapaṭibaddhaṃ sakalasarīraṃ byāpetvā ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: 69.As to bile, the free bile, which is bound up with the life faculty, is to be found soaking the whole body,

rows.pali: Baddhapittaṃ pittakosake ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: while the local bile is to be found in the bile container (gall-bladder).

rows.pali: Tattha yathā pūvaṃ byāpetvā ṭhite tele na pūvaṃ jānāti telaṃ maṃ byāpetvā ṭhitanti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when oil has soaked a cake, the cake does not know, “Oil soaks me,”

rows.pali: Napi telaṃ jānāti ahaṃ pūvaṃ byāpetvā ṭhitanti, evameva na sarīraṃ jānāti abaddhapittaṃ maṃ byāpetvā ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the oil know, “I soak a cake,” so too, the body does not know, “Free bile soaks me,”

rows.pali: Napi abaddhapittaṃ jānāti ahaṃ sarīraṃ byāpetvā ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the free bile know, “I soak a body.”

rows.pali: Yathā vassodakena puṇṇe kosātakikosake na kosātakikosako jānāti mayi vassodakaṃ ṭhitanti.
rows.english: And just as, when a kosāṭakī (loofah) creeper bladder is filled with rain water, the kosāṭakī creeper bladder does not know, “Rain water is in me,”

rows.pali: Napi vassodakaṃ jānāti ahaṃ kosātakikosake ṭhitanti, evameva na pittakosako jānāti mayi baddhapittaṃ ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the rain water know, “I am in a kosāṭakī creeper bladder,” so too, the bile bladder does not know, “Local bile is in me,”

rows.pali: Napi baddhapittaṃ jānāti ahaṃ pittakosake ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the local bile know, “I am in a bile bladder.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti pittaṃ nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto yūsabhūto ābandhanākāro āpodhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called bile is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, liquid water element in the mode of cohesion.

rows.pali: 332.Semhaṃ ekapatthapūrappamāṇaṃ udarapaṭale ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: 70.Phlegm is to be found on the surface of the stomach and measures a bowlful.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā upari sañjātapheṇapaṭalāya candanikāya na candanikā jānāti mayi pheṇapaṭalaṃ ṭhitanti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when a cesspool has a surface of froth, the cesspool does not know, “A surface of froth is on me,”

rows.pali: Napi pheṇapaṭalaṃ jānāti ahaṃ candanikāya ṭhitanti, evameva na udarapaṭalaṃ jānāti mayi semhaṃ ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the surface of froth [360] know, “I am on a cesspool,” so too, the surface of the stomach does not know, “Phlegm is on me”

rows.pali: Napi semhaṃ jānāti ahaṃ udarapaṭale ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the phlegm know, “I am on the surface of a stomach.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti semhaṃ nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto yūsabhūto ābandhanākāro āpodhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called phlegm is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, liquid water element in the mode of cohesion.

rows.pali: 333.Pubbo anibaddhokāso yattha yattheva khāṇukaṇṭakappaharaṇaaggijālādīhi abhihate sarīrappadese lohitaṃ saṇṭhahitvā paccati, gaṇḍapīḷakādayo vā uppajjanti, tattha tattha tiṭṭhati.
rows.english: 71. Pus has no fixed location. It is to be found wherever the blood stagnates and goes bad in a part of the body damaged by wounds caused by splinters and thorns, and by burns due to fire, or where boils, carbuncles, etc., appear.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā pharasuppahārādivasena paggharitaniyyāse rukkhe na rukkhassa pahārādippadesā jānanti amhesu niyyāso ṭhitoti, napi niyyāso jānāti ahaṃ rukkhassa pahārādippadesesu ṭhitoti, evameva na sarīrassa khāṇukaṇṭakādīhi abhihatappadesā jānanti amhesu pubbo ṭhitoti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when a tree oozes gum through being hit by, say, an axe, the parts of the tree that have been hit do not know, “Gum is in us,” nor does the gum know, “I am in a part of a tree that has been hit,” so too, the parts of the body wounded by splinters, thorns, etc., do not know, “Pus is in us,”

rows.pali: Napi pubbo jānāti ahaṃ tesu padesesu ṭhitoti.
rows.english: nor does the pus know, “I am in such places.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti pubbo nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto yūsabhūto ābandhanākāro āpodhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called pus is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, liquid water element in the mode of cohesion.

rows.pali: 334.Lohitesu saṃsaraṇalohitaṃ pittaṃ viya sakalasarīraṃ byāpetvā ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: 72.As to blood, the mobile blood is to be found, like the bile, soaking the whole body.

rows.pali: Sannicitalohitaṃ yakanaṭṭhānassa heṭṭhābhāgaṃ pūretvā ekapatthapūramattaṃ vakkahadayayakanapapphāsāni tementaṃ ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: The stored blood, is to be found filling the lower part of the liver’s site to the extent of a bowlful, wetting the kidney, heart, liver and lungs.

rows.pali: Tattha saṃsaraṇalohite abaddhapittasadisova vinicchayo.
rows.english: Herein, the definition of the mobile blood is similar to that of the free bile.

rows.pali: Itaraṃ pana yathā jajjarakapāle ovaṭṭhe udake heṭṭhā leḍḍukhaṇḍādīni temayamāne na leḍḍukhaṇḍādīni jānanti mayaṃ udakena temiyamānāti.
rows.english: But as to the other, just as, when rain water seeps through an old pot and wets clods and stumps below, the clods and stumps do not know, “We are being wetted with water,”

rows.pali: Napi udakaṃ jānāti ahaṃ leḍḍukhaṇḍādīni tememīti, evameva na yakanassa heṭṭhābhāgaṭṭhānaṃ vakkādīni vā jānanti mayi lohitaṃ ṭhitaṃ amhe vā temayamānaṃ ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the water know, “I am wetting clods and stumps,” so too, the lower part of the liver’s site, or the kidneys, etc., respectively do not know, “Blood is in me,” or “We are being wetted,”

rows.pali: Napi lohitaṃ jānāti ahaṃ yakanassa heṭṭhābhāgaṃ pūretvā vakkādīni temayamānaṃ ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the blood know, “I fill the lower part of a liver’s site, am wetting a kidney, and so on.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti lohitaṃ nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto yūsabhūto ābandhanākāro āpodhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called blood is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, liquid water element in the mode of cohesion.

rows.pali: 335.Sedo aggisantāpādikālesu kesalomakūpavivarāni pūretvā tiṭṭhati ceva paggharati ca.
rows.english: 73.Sweat is to be found filling the openings of the pores of the head hairs and body hairs when there is heat due to fires, etc., and it trickles out of them.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā udakā abbūḷhamattesu bhisamuḷālakumudanāḷakalāpesu na bhisādikalāpavivarāni jānanti amhehi udakaṃ paggharatīti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when [361] bunches of lily bud stems and lotus stalks are pulled up out of water, the openings in the bunches of lilies, etc., do not know, “Water trickles from us,”

rows.pali: Napi bhisādikalāpavivarehi paggharantaṃ udakaṃ jānāti ahaṃ bhisādikalāpavivarehi paggharāmīti, evameva na kesalomakūpavivarāni jānanti amhehi sedo paggharatīti.
rows.english: nor does the water trickling from the openings in the bunches of lilies, etc., know, “I am trickling from openings in bunches of lilies, etc.,” so too, the openings of the pores of the head hairs and body hairs do not know, “Sweat trickles from us,”

rows.pali: Napi sedo jānāti ahaṃ kesalomakūpavivarehi paggharāmīti.
rows.english: nor does the sweat know, “I trickle from openings of pores of head hairs and body hairs.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti sedo nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto yūsabhūto ābandhanākāro āpodhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called sweat is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, liquid water element in the mode of cohesion.

rows.pali: 336.Medo thūlassa sakalasarīraṃ pharitvā kisassa jaṅghamaṃsādīni nissāya ṭhito patthinnasineho.
rows.english: 74.Fat is the thick unguent to be found pervading the whole body of one who is stout, and on the shank flesh, etc., of one who is lean.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā haliddipilotikapaṭicchanne maṃsapuñje na maṃsapuñjo jānāti maṃ nissāya haliddipilotikā ṭhitāti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when a heap of meat is covered by a yellow rag, the heap of meat does not know, “A yellow rag is next to me,”

rows.pali: Napi haliddipilotikā jānāti ahaṃ maṃsapuñjaṃ nissāya ṭhitāti, evameva na sakalasarīre jaṅghādīsu vā maṃsaṃ jānāti maṃ nissāya medo ṭhitoti.
rows.english: nor does the yellow rag know, “I am next to a heap of meat,” so too, the flesh to be found on the whole body, or on the shanks, etc., does not know, “Fat is next to me,”

rows.pali: Napi medo jānāti ahaṃ sakalasarīre jaṅghādīsu vā maṃsaṃ nissāya ṭhitoti.
rows.english: nor does the fat know,”I am next to flesh on a whole body, or on the shanks, and so on.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti medo nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto patthinnayūso ābandhanākāro āpodhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called fat is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, thick-liquid water element in the mode of cohesion.

rows.pali: 337.Assuyadā sañjāyati tadā akkhikūpake pūretvā tiṭṭhati vā paggharati vā.
rows.english: 75.Tears, when produced, are to be found filling the eye sockets or trickling out of them.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā udakapuṇṇesu taruṇatālaṭṭhikūpakesu na taruṇatālaṭṭhikūpakā jānanti amhesu udakaṃ ṭhitanti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when the sockets of young palm kernels are filled with water, the sockets of the young palm kernels do not know, “Water is in us,”

rows.pali: Napi taruṇatālaṭṭhikūpakesu udakaṃ jānāti ahaṃ taruṇatālaṭṭhikūpakesu ṭhitanti, evameva na akkhikūpakā jānanti amhesu assu ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the water in the sockets of the young palm kernels know, “I am in sockets of young palm kernels,” so too, the eye sockets do not know, “Tears are in us,”

rows.pali: Napi assu jānāti ahaṃ akkhikūpakesu ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor do the tears know, “We are in eye sockets.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti assu nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto yūsabhūto ābandhanākāro āpodhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called tears is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, liquid water element in the mode of cohesion.

rows.pali: 338.Vasāaggisantāpādikāle hatthatalahatthapiṭṭhipādatalapādapiṭṭhi nāsāpuṭanalāṭaaṃsakūṭesu ṭhitavilīnasneho.
rows.english: 76.Grease is the melted unguent to be found on the palms and backs of the hands, on the soles and backs of the feet, on the nose and forehead and on the points of the shoulders, when heated by fire, and so on.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā pakkhittatele ācāme na ācāmo jānāti maṃ telaṃ ajjhottharitvā ṭhitanti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when rice gruel has oil put on it, the rice gruel does not know, “Oil is spread over me,”

rows.pali: Napi telaṃ jānāti ahaṃ ācāmaṃ ajjhottharitvā ṭhitanti, evameva na hatthatalādippadeso jānāti maṃ vasā ajjhottharitvā ṭhitāti.
rows.english: nor does the oil know, “I am spread over rice gruel,” so too, the place consisting of the palm of the hand, etc., [362] does not know, “Grease is spread over me,”

rows.pali: Napi vasā jānāti ahaṃ hatthatalādippadesaṃ ajjhottharitvā ṭhitāti.
rows.english: nor does the grease know, “I am spread over places consisting of the palm of the hand, and so on.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti vasā nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto yūsabhūto ābandhanākāro āpodhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called grease is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, liquid water element in the mode of cohesion.

rows.pali: 339.Kheḷo tathārūpe kheḷuppattipaccaye sati ubhohi kapolapassehi orohitvā jivhātale tiṭṭhati.
rows.english: 77.Spittle is to be found on the surface of the tongue after it has descended from the cheeks on both sides, when there is a condition for the arising of spittle.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā abbocchinnaudakanissande nadītīrakūpake na kūpatalaṃ jānāti mayi udakaṃ santiṭṭhatīti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when a hollow in a river bank is constantly oozing with water, the surface of the hollow does not know, “Water lies on me,”

rows.pali: Napi udakaṃ jānāti ahaṃ kūpatale santiṭṭhāmīti, evameva na jivhātalaṃ jānāti mayi ubhohi kapolapassehi orohitvā kheḷo ṭhitoti.
rows.english: nor does the water know, “I lie on the surface of a hollow,” so too, the surface of the tongue does not know, “Spittle that has descended from cheeks on both sides is on me,”

rows.pali: Napi kheḷo jānāti ahaṃ ubhohi kapolapassehi orohitvā jivhātale ṭhitoti.
rows.english: nor does the spittle know, “I have descended from cheeks on both sides and am on the surface of a tongue.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti kheḷo nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto yūsabhūto ābandhanākāro āpodhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called spittle is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, liquid water element in the mode of cohesion.

rows.pali: 340.Siṅghāṇikāyadā sañjāyati, tadā nāsāpuṭe pūretvā tiṭṭhati vā paggharati vā.
rows.english: 78.Snot, when produced, is to be found filling the nostrils or trickling out of them.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā pūtidadhibharitāya sippikāya na sippikā jānāti mayi pūtidadhi ṭhitanti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when a bag28 is loaded with rotting curd, the bag does not know, “Rotting curd is in me,”

rows.pali: Napi pūtidadhi jānāti ahaṃ sippikāya ṭhitanti, evameva na nāsāpuṭā jānanti amhesu siṅghāṇikā ṭhitāti.
rows.english: nor does the rotting curd know, “I am in a bag,” so too, the nostrils do not know, “Snot is in us,”

rows.pali: Napi siṅghāṇikā jānāti ahaṃ nāsāpuṭesu ṭhitāti.
rows.english: nor does the snot know, “I am in nostrils.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti siṅghāṇikā nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto yūsabhūto ābandhanākāro āpodhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called snot is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, liquid water element in the mode of cohesion.

rows.pali: 341.Lasikā aṭṭhikasandhīnaṃ abbhañjanakiccaṃ sādhayamānā asītisatasandhīsu ṭhitā.
rows.english: 79.Oil of the joints is to be found in the hundred and eighty joints serving the function of lubricating the joints of the bones.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā telabbhañjite akkhe na akkho jānāti maṃ telaṃ abbhañjitvā ṭhitanti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when an axle is lubricated with oil, the axle does not know, “Oil lubricates me,”

rows.pali: Napi telaṃ jānāti ahaṃ akkhaṃ abbhañjitvā ṭhitanti, evameva na asītisatasandhayo jānanti lasikā amhe abbhañjitvā ṭhitāti.
rows.english: nor does the oil know, “I lubricate an axle,” so too, the hundred and eighty joints do not know, “Oil of the joints lubricates us,”

rows.pali: Napi lasikā jānāti ahaṃ asītisatasandhayo abbhañjitvā ṭhitāti.
rows.english: nor does the oil of the joints know, “I lubricate a hundred and eighty joints.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti lasikā nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto yūsabhūto ābandhanākāro āpodhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called oil of the joints is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, liquid water element in the mode of cohesion.

rows.pali: 342.Muttaṃ vatthissa abbhantare ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: 80.Urine is to be found inside the bladder.

rows.pali: Tattha yathā candanikāya pakkhitte amukhe ravaṇaghaṭe na ravaṇaghaṭo jānāti mayi candanikāraso ṭhitoti.
rows.english: Herein, just as, when a porous pot is put upside down in a cesspool, the porous pot does not know, “Cesspool filtrate is in me,”

rows.pali: Napi candanikāraso jānāti ahaṃ ravaṇaghaṭe ṭhitoti, evameva na vatthi jānāti mayi muttaṃ ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the cesspool filtrate know, “I am in a porous pot,” so too, the bladder does not know, [363] “Urine is in me,”

rows.pali: Napi muttaṃ jānāti ahaṃ vatthimhi ṭhitanti.
rows.english: nor does the urine know, “I am in a bladder.”

rows.pali: Aññamaññaṃ ābhogapaccavekkhaṇarahitā ete dhammā.
rows.english: These things are devoid of mutual concern and reviewing.

rows.pali: Iti muttaṃ nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto yūsabhūto ābandhanākāro āpodhātūti.
rows.english: So what is called urine is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being, liquid water element in the mode of cohesion.

rows.pali: 343.Evaṃ kesādīsu manasikāraṃ pavattetvā yena santappati, ayaṃ imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto paripācanākāro tejodhātūti, yena jīrīyati, yena pariḍayhati, yena asitapītakhāyitasāyitaṃ sammā pariṇāmaṃ gacchati, ayaṃ imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto paripācanākāro tejodhātūti evaṃ tejokoṭṭhāsesu manasikāro pavattetabbo.
rows.english: 81.When he has given his attention in this way to the body hairs, etc., he should then give his attention to the [four] fire components thus: That whereby one is warmed— this is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being; it is fire element in the mode of maturing (ripening). That whereby one ages … That whereby one burns up … That whereby what is eaten, drunk, chewed and tasted becomes completely digested— this is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being; it is fire element in the mode of maturing (ripening).

rows.pali: 344.Tato uddhaṅgame vāte uddhaṅgamavasena pariggahetvā adhogame adhogamavasena, kucchisaye kucchisayavasena, koṭṭhāsaye koṭṭhāsayavasena, aṅgamaṅgānusārimhi aṅgamaṅgānusārivasena, assāsapassāse assāsapassāsavasena pariggahetvā uddhaṅgamā vātā nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto vitthambhanākāro vāyodhātūti, adhogamā vātā nāma, kucchisayā vātā nāma, koṭṭhāsayā vātā nāma, aṅgamaṅgānusārino vātā nāma, assāsapassāsā vātā nāma imasmiṃ sarīre pāṭiyekko koṭṭhāso acetano abyākato suñño nissatto vitthambhanākāro vāyodhātūti evaṃ vāyokoṭṭhāsesu manasikāro pavattetabbo.
rows.english: 82. After that, having discovered the up-going winds (forces) as upgoing, the down-going winds (forces) as down-going, the winds (forces) in the belly as in the belly, the winds (forces) in the bowels as in the bowels, the winds (forces) that course through all the limbs as coursing through all the limbs, and in-breath and out- breath as in-breath and out-breath, he should give his attention to these [six] air components in this way: What is called up-going winds (forces) is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being; it is air element in the mode of distending. What is called down-going winds (forces) … What is called winds (forces) in the belly … What is called winds (forces) in the bowels … What is called winds (forces) that course through all the limbs … What is called in-breath and out-breath is a particular component of this body, without thought, indeterminate, void, not a living being; it is air element in the mode of distending.

rows.pali: Tassevaṃ pavattamanasikārassa dhātuyo pākaṭā honti.
rows.english: 83.As he gives his attention in this way the elements become evident to him.

rows.pali: Tā punappunaṃ āvajjato manasikaroto vuttanayeneva upacārasamādhi uppajjati.
rows.english: As he adverts and gives attention to them again and again access concentration arises in him in the way already described.

rows.pali: 345.Yassa pana evaṃ bhāvayato kammaṭṭhānaṃ na ijjhati, tena salakkhaṇasaṅkhepato bhāvetabbaṃ.
rows.english: [(3) WITH CHARACTERISTICS IN BRIEF] 84.But if his meditation subject is still not successful when he gives his attention to it in this way, then he should develop it with characteristics in brief.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Vīsatiyā koṭṭhāsesu thaddhalakkhaṇaṃ pathavīdhātūti vavatthapetabbaṃ.
rows.english: In the twenty components the characteristic of stiffenedness should be defined as the earth element,

rows.pali: Tattheva ābandhanalakkhaṇaṃ āpodhātūti.
rows.english: and the characteristic of cohesion, which is there too, as the water element,

rows.pali: Paripācanalakkhaṇaṃ tejodhātūti.
rows.english: and the characteristic of maturing (ripening), which is there too, as the fire element,

rows.pali: Vitthambhanalakkhaṇaṃ vāyodhātūti.
rows.english: and the characteristic of distension, which is there too, as the air element.

rows.pali: Dvādasasu koṭṭhāsesu ābandhanalakkhaṇaṃ āpodhātūti vavatthapetabbaṃ.
rows.english: In the twelve components the characteristic of cohesion should be defined as the water

rows.pali: Tattheva paripācanalakkhaṇaṃ tejodhātūti.
rows.english: element, the characteristic of maturing (ripening), which is there too, as the fire element,

rows.pali: Vitthambhanalakkhaṇaṃ vāyodhātūti.
rows.english: the characteristic of distension, which is there too, as the air element,

rows.pali: Thaddhalakkhaṇaṃ pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: and the characteristic of stiffenedness, which is there too, as the earth element.

rows.pali: Catūsu koṭṭhāsesu paripācanalakkhaṇaṃ tejodhātūti vavatthapetabbaṃ.
rows.english: In the four components the characteristic of maturing (ripening) should be defined as the fire element,

rows.pali: Tena avinibhuttaṃ vitthambhanalakkhaṇaṃ vāyodhātūti.
rows.english: the characteristic of distension unresolvable (inseparable) from it as the air element, [364]

rows.pali: Thaddhalakkhaṇaṃ pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: the characteristic of stiffenedness as the earth element,

rows.pali: Ābandhanalakkhaṇaṃ āpodhātūti.
rows.english: and the characteristic of cohesion as the water element.

rows.pali: Chasu koṭṭhāsesu vitthambhanalakkhaṇaṃ vāyodhātūti vavatthapetabbaṃ.
rows.english: In the six components the characteristic of distension should be defined as the air element,

rows.pali: Tattheva thaddhalakkhaṇaṃ pathavīdhātūti.
rows.english: the characteristic of stiffenedness there too as the earth element,

rows.pali: Ābandhanalakkhaṇaṃ āpodhātūti.
rows.english: the characteristic of cohesion as the water element,

rows.pali: Paripācanalakkhaṇaṃ tejodhātūti.
rows.english: and the characteristic of maturing (ripening) as the fire element.

rows.pali: Tassevaṃ vavatthāpayato dhātuyo pākaṭā honti.
rows.english: As he defines them in this way the elements become evident to him.

rows.pali: Tā punappunaṃ āvajjato manasikaroto vuttanayeneva upacārasamādhi uppajjati.
rows.english: As he adverts to them and gives attention to them again and again access concentration arises in him in the way already stated.

rows.pali: 346.Yassa pana evampi bhāvayato kammaṭṭhānaṃ na ijjhati, tena salakkhaṇavibhattito bhāvetabbaṃ.
rows.english: [(4) WITH CHARACTERISTICS BY ANALYSIS] 85.However, if he still does not succeed with his meditation subject when he gives his attention to it in this way, then he should develop it with characteristics by analysis.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Pubbe vuttanayeneva kesādayo pariggahetvā kesamhi thaddhalakkhaṇaṃ pathavīdhātūti vavatthapetabbaṃ.
rows.english: After discerning head hairs, etc., in the way already described, the characteristic of stiffenedness in head hairs should be defined as the earth element,

rows.pali: Tattheva ābandhanalakkhaṇaṃ āpodhātūti.
rows.english: the characteristic of cohesion there too as the water element,

rows.pali: Paripācanalakkhaṇaṃ tejodhātūti.
rows.english: the characteristic of maturing (ripening) as the fire element,

rows.pali: Vitthambhanalakkhaṇaṃ vāyodhātūti.
rows.english: and the characteristic of distension as the air element.

rows.pali: Evaṃ sabbakoṭṭhāsesu ekekasmiṃ koṭṭhāse catasso catasso dhātuyo vavatthapetabbā.
rows.english: The four elements should be defined in this way in the case of each component.

rows.pali: Tassevaṃ vavatthāpayato dhātuyo pākaṭā honti.
rows.english: As he defines them in this way the elements become evident to him.

rows.pali: Tā punappunaṃ āvajjato manasikaroto vuttanayeneva upacārasamādhi uppajjati.
rows.english: As he adverts and gives attention to them again and again access concentration arises in him in the way already described.

rows.pali: Apica kho pana vacanatthato, kalāpato, cuṇṇato, lakkhaṇādito, samuṭṭhānato, nānattekattato, vinibbhogāvinibbhogato, sabhāgavisabhāgato, ajjhattikabāhiravisesato, saṅgahato, paccayato, asamannāhārato, paccayavibhāgatoti imehipi ākārehi dhātuyo manasikātabbā.
rows.english: 86.In addition, attention should be given to the elements in the following ways: (1) as to word meaning, (2) by groups, (3) by particles, (4) by characteristic, etc., (5) as to how originated, (6) as to variety and unity, (7) as to resolution (separability) and non-resolution (inseparability), (8) as to the similar and the dissimilar, (9) as to distinction between internal and external, (10) as to inclusion, (11) as to condition, (12) as to lack of conscious reaction, (13) as to analysis of conditions. 87.

rows.pali: 347.Tattha vacanatthato manasikarontena patthaṭattā pathavī.
rows.english: 1. Herein, one who gives his attention to them as to word meaning should do so separately and generally thus: [separately] it is earth (pathavī) because it is spread out (patthaṭa);

rows.pali: Appoti āpiyati appāyatīti vā āpo.
rows.english: it flows (appoti) or it glides (āpiyati) or it satisfies (appāyati), thus it is water (āpo);

rows.pali: Tejatīti tejo.
rows.english: it heats (tejati), thus it is fire (tejo);

rows.pali: Vāyatīti vāyo.
rows.english: it blows (vāyati), thus it is air (vāyo).

rows.pali: Avisesena pana salakkhaṇadhāraṇato dukkhādānato dukkhādhānato ca dhātūti.
rows.english: But without differentiation they are elements (dhātu) because of bearing (dhāraṇa) their own characteristics, because of grasping (ādāna) suffering, and because of sorting out (ādhāna) suffering (see XV.19).29

rows.pali: Evaṃ visesasāmaññavasena vacanatthato manasikātabbā.
rows.english: This is how they should be given attention as to word meaning.

rows.pali: 348.Kalāpatoti yā ayaṃ kesā lomātiādinā nayena vīsatiyā ākārehi pathavīdhātu, pittaṃ semhanti ca ādinā nayena dvādasahākārehi āpodhātu niddiṭṭhā, tattha yasmā –
rows.english: 88. 2. By groups: there is the earth element described under the twenty aspects (modes) beginning with head hairs, body hairs, and also the water element described under the twelve (modes) aspects beginning with bile, phlegm, etc. Now, as to these:

rows.pali: Tasmā kesāpi aṭṭhadhammakalāpamattameva.
rows.english: Consequently, head hairs are only a mere group of eight states.

rows.pali: Tathā lomādayoti.
rows.english: Likewise, body hairs, [365] and the rest.

rows.pali: Yo panettha kammasamuṭṭhāno koṭṭhāso, so jīvitindriyena ca bhāvena ca saddhiṃ dasadhammakalāpopi hoti.
rows.english: A component here that is kamma-originated is a group of ten states, [that is to say, the former eight] together with the life faculty and sex.

rows.pali: Ussadavasena pana pathavīdhātu āpodhātūti saṅkhaṃ gato.
rows.english: But it is on account of respective prominence [of stiffenedness or cohesion] that it comes to be styled “earth element” or “water element.”

rows.pali: Evaṃ kalāpato manasikātabbā.
rows.english: This is how they should be given attention to “by groups.”

rows.pali: 349.Cuṇṇatoti imasmiṃ hi sarīre majjhimena pamāṇena pariggayhamānā paramāṇubhedasañcuṇṇā sukhumarajabhūtā pathavīdhātu doṇamattā siyā.
rows.english: 89. 3. By particles: in this body the earth element taken as reduced to fine dust and powdered to the size of the smallest atom31 might amount to an average doṇa measure full;

rows.pali: Sā tato upaḍḍhappamāṇāya āpodhātuyā saṅgahitā, tejodhātuyā anupālitā vāyodhātuyā vitthambhitā na vikiriyati na viddhaṃsiyati, avikiriyamānā aviddhaṃsiyamānā anekavidhaṃ itthipurisaliṅgādibhāvavikappaṃ upagacchati, aṇuthūladīgharassathirakathinādibhāvañca pakāseti.
rows.english: and that is held together32 by the water element measuring half as much. Being maintained33 by the fire element, and distended by the air element, it does not get scattered or dissipated. Instead of getting scattered or dissipated, it arrives at the alternative states of the female and male sex, etc., and manifests smallness, bigness, length, shortness, toughness, rigidity, and so on.

rows.pali: Yūsagatā ābandhanākārabhūtā panettha āpodhātu pathavīpatiṭṭhitā tejānupālitā vāyovitthambhitā na paggharati na parissavati, apaggharamānā aparissavamānā pīṇitapīṇitabhāvaṃ dasseti.
rows.english: 90. The liquid water element that is the mode of cohesion, being founded on earth, maintained by fire, and distended by air, does not trickle or run away.34 Instead of trickling or running away it provides continued refreshments.35

rows.pali: Asitapītādipācakā cettha usumākārabhūtā uṇhattalakkhaṇā tejodhātu pathavīpatiṭṭhitā āposaṅgahitā vāyovitthambhitā imaṃ kāyaṃ paripāceti, vaṇṇasampattiñcassa āvahati.
rows.english: 91.And here the fire element that cooks what is eaten, drunk, etc., and is the mode of warming and has the characteristic of heat, being established on earth, held together by water, and distended by air, maintains this body and ensures its proper appearance.

rows.pali: Tāya ca pana paripācito ayaṃ kāyo na pūtibhāvaṃ dasseti.
rows.english: And this body, being maintained by it, shows no putrefaction.

rows.pali: Aṅgamaṅgānusaṭā cettha samudīraṇavitthambhanalakkhaṇā vāyodhātu pathavīpatiṭṭhitā āposaṅgahitā tejānupālitā imaṃ kāyaṃ vitthambheti.
rows.english: 92.The air element that courses through all the limbs and has the characteristic of moving and distending, being founded upon earth, held together by water, and maintained by fire, distends this body.

rows.pali: Tāya ca pana vitthambhito ayaṃ kāyo na paripatati, ujukaṃ saṇṭhāti.
rows.english: And this body, being distended by the latter kind of air, does not collapse, but stands erect,

rows.pali: Aparāya vāyodhātuyā samabbhāhato gamanaṭṭhānanisajjāsayanairiyāpathesu viññattiṃ dasseti, samiñjeti, sampasāreti, hatthapādaṃ lāḷeti.
rows.english: and being propelled36 by the other [motile] air, it shows intimation and it flexes and extends and it wriggles the hands and feet, doing so in the postures comprising of walking, standing, sitting and lying down.

rows.pali: Evametaṃ itthipurisādibhāvena bālajanavañcanaṃ māyārūpasadisaṃ dhātuyantaṃ pavattatīti evaṃ cuṇṇato manasikātabbā.
rows.english: So this mechanism of elements carries on like a magic trick, deceiving foolish people with the male and female sex and so on. This is how they should be given attention by particles.

rows.pali: 350.Lakkhaṇāditoti pathavīdhātu kiṃ lakkhaṇā, kiṃ rasā, kiṃ paccupaṭṭhānāti evaṃ catassopi dhātuyo āvajjetvā pathavīdhātu kakkhaḷattalakkhaṇā, patiṭṭhānarasā, sampaṭicchanapaccupaṭṭhānā.
rows.english: 93. 4. As to characteristic, etc.: he should advert to the four elements in this way: “The earth element—what are its characteristic, function, manifestation?” [defining them in this way]: The earth element has the characteristic of hardness. Its function is to act as a foundation. It is manifested as receiving.

rows.pali: Āpodhātu paggharaṇalakkhaṇā, brūhanarasā, saṅgahapaccupaṭṭhānā.
rows.english: The water element has the characteristic of trickling. Its function is to intensify. It is manifested as holding together.

rows.pali: Tejodhātu uṇhattalakkhaṇā, paripācanarasā, maddavānuppadānapaccupaṭṭhānā.
rows.english: The fire element has the characteristic of heat. Its function is to mature (maintain). It is manifested as a continued supply of softness.

rows.pali: Vāyodhātu vitthambhanalakkhaṇā, samudīraṇarasā.
rows.english: The air element has the characteristic of distending. Its function is to cause motion. It is manifested as conveying.37

rows.pali: Abhinīhārapaccupaṭṭhānāti evaṃ lakkhaṇādito manasikātabbā.
rows.english: This is how they should be given attention to by characteristic, and so on.

rows.pali: 351.Samuṭṭhānatoti ye ime pathavīdhātuādīnaṃ vitthārato dassanavasena kesādayo dvācattālīsa koṭṭhāsā dassitā.
rows.english: 94. 5. As to how originated: among the forty-two components beginning with head hairs shown in the detailed treatment of the earth element, etc.,

rows.pali: Tesu udariyaṃ karīsaṃ pubbo muttanti ime cattāro koṭṭhāsā utusamuṭṭhānāva.
rows.english: the four consisting of gorge, dung, pus, and urine are temperature-originated only;

rows.pali: Assu sedo kheḷo siṅghāṇikāti ime cattāro utucittasamuṭṭhānā.
rows.english: the four consisting of tears, sweat, spittle, and snot are temperature-originated and consciousness-originated only.

rows.pali: Asitādiparipācako tejo kammasamuṭṭhānova.
rows.english: The fire that cooks what is eaten, etc., is kamma- originated only;

rows.pali: Assāsapassāsā cittasamuṭṭhānāva.
rows.english: in-breath and out-breath are consciousness-originated only;

rows.pali: Avasesā sabbepi catusamuṭṭhānāti evaṃ samuṭṭhānato manasikātabbā.
rows.english: all the rest are of fourfold origination. This is how they should be given attention as to how originated.

rows.pali: 352.Nānattekattatoti sabbāsampi dhātūnaṃ salakkhaṇādito nānattaṃ.
rows.english: 95. 6. As to variety and unity: there is variety in the specific characteristics, etc., of all the elements;

rows.pali: Aññāneva hi pathavīdhātuyā lakkhaṇarasapaccupaṭṭhānāni.
rows.english: for the characteristic, function, and manifestation of the earth element is one,

rows.pali: Aññāni āpodhātuādīnaṃ.
rows.english: and those of the water element, etc., are different.

rows.pali: Evaṃ lakkhaṇādivasena pana kammasamuṭṭhānādivasena ca nānattabhūtānampi etāsaṃ rūpamahābhūtadhātudhammaaniccādivasena ekattaṃ hoti.
rows.english: But there is unity in them as materiality, great primary, element, state (dhamma), imperma- nence, etc., notwithstanding the fact that they are various according to [specific] characteristic, etc., and according to origination by kamma and so on.

rows.pali: Sabbāpi hi dhātuyo ruppanalakkhaṇaṃ anatītattā rūpāni.
rows.english: 96.All these elements are “instances of materiality” (rūpāni) because they do not exceed the characteristic of “being molested” (ruppana).

rows.pali: Mahantapātubhāvādīhi kāraṇehi mahābhūtāni.
rows.english: They are “great primaries” (mahābhūta) by reason of “great manifestation,” and so on.

rows.pali: Mahantapātubhāvādīhīti etā hi dhātuyo mahantapātubhāvato, mahābhūtasāmaññato, mahāparihārato, mahāvikārato, mahattā bhūtattā cāti imehi kāraṇehi mahābhūtānīti vuccanti.
rows.english: “By reason of ‘great manifestation,’ and so on” means that these elements are called “great primaries” for the following reasons, namely: (a) manifestation of greatness; (b) likeness to great creatures; (c) great maintenance; (d) great alteration; and (e) because they are great and because they are entities.

rows.pali: Tattha mahantapātubhāvatoti etāni hi anupādinnasantānepi upādinnasantānepi mahantāni pātubhūtāni.
rows.english: 97.Herein, (a) manifestation of greatness: they are manifested as great both in a continuity that is not clung to (acquired through kamma) and in a continuity that is clung to.

rows.pali: Tesaṃ anupādinnasantāne –
rows.english: For their manifestation of greatness in a continuity that is not clung to is given

rows.pali: Upādinnasantānepi macchakacchapadevadānavādisarīravasena mahantāneva pātubhūtāni.
rows.english: And they are manifested on a great scale also in a continuity that is clung to, for instance, in the bodies of fishes, turtles, deities, Dānava demons, and so on.

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ "santi, bhikkhave, mahāsamudde yojanasatikāpi attabhāvā"tiādi.
rows.english: For this is said: “Bhikkhus, there are individual creatures of a hundred leagues in the great ocean” (A IV 207), and so on.

rows.pali: Mahābhūtasāmaññatoti etāni hi yathā māyākāro amaṇiṃyeva udakaṃ maṇiṃ katvā dasseti, asuvaṇṇaṃyeva leḍḍuṃ suvaṇṇaṃ katvā dasseti.
rows.english: 98.(b) Likeness to great creatures: just as a magician turns water that is not crystal into crystal, and turns a clod that is not gold into gold, and shows them,

rows.pali: Yathā ca sayaṃ neva yakkho na yakkhī samāno yakkhabhāvampi yakkhibhāvampi dasseti, evameva sayaṃ anīlāneva hutvā nīlaṃ upādārūpaṃ dassenti, apītāni alohitāni anodātāneva hutvā odātaṃ upādārūpaṃ dassentīti māyākāramahābhūtasāmaññato mahābhūtāni.
rows.english: and being himself neither a spirit nor a bird, shows himself as a spirit or a bird, so too, being themselves not blue-black, they turn themselves into blue-black derived materiality, being themselves not yellow … not red … not white, [367] they turn themselves into white derived materiality and show that. In this way they are “great primaries” (mahābhūta) in being like the great creatures (mahābhūta) of a magician.38

rows.pali: Yathā ca yakkhādīni mahābhūtāni yaṃ gaṇhanti, neva nesaṃ tassa anto na bahi ṭhānaṃ upalabbhati, na ca taṃ nissāya na tiṭṭhanti, evameva tānipi neva aññamaññassa anto na bahi ṭhitāni hutvā upalabbhanti, na ca aññamaññaṃ nissāya na tiṭṭhantīti acinteyyaṭṭhānatāya yakkhādimahābhūtasāmaññatopi mahābhūtāni.
rows.english: 99. And just as, whomsoever the great creatures such as the spirits (yakkha) grasp hold of (possess), they have no standing place either inside him or outside him and yet they have no standing independently of him, so too, these elements are not found to stand either inside or outside each other yet they have no standing independently of one another. Thus they are also great primaries (mahābhūta) in being equal to the great creatures (mahābhūta) such as the spirits because they have no thinkable standing place [relative to each other].

rows.pali: Yathā ca yakkhinīsaṅkhātāni mahābhūtāni manāpehi vaṇṇasaṇṭhānavikkhepehi attano bhayānakabhāvaṃ paṭicchādetvā satte vañcenti, evameva etānipi itthipurisasarīrādīsu manāpena chavivaṇṇena manāpena attano aṅgapaccaṅgasaṇṭhānena manāpena ca hatthapādaṅgulibhamukavikkhepena attano kakkhaḷattādibhedaṃ sarasalakkhaṇaṃ paṭicchādetvā bālajanaṃ vañcenti, attano sabhāvaṃ daṭṭhuṃ na dentīti vañcakattena yakkhinīmahābhūtasāmaññatopi mahābhūtāni.
rows.english: 100. And just as the great creatures known as female spirits (yakkhinī) conceal their own fearfulness with a pleasing colour, shape and gesture to deceive beings, so too, these elements conceal each their own characteristic and function classed as hardness, etc., by means of a pleasing skin colour of women’s and men’s bodies, etc., and pleasing shapes of limbs and pleasing gestures of fingers, toes and eyebrows, and they deceive simple people by concealing their own functions and characteristics beginning with hardness and do not allow their individual essences to be seen. Thus they are great primaries (mahābhūta) in being equal to the great creatures (mahābhūta), the female spirits, since they are deceivers.

rows.pali: Mahāparihāratoti mahantehi paccayehi pariharitabbato.
rows.english: 101.(c) Great maintenance: this is because they have to be sustained by the great requisites.

rows.pali: Etāni hi divase divase upanetabbattā mahantehi ghāsacchādanādīhi bhūtāni pavattānīti mahābhūtāni.
rows.english: For these elements are great primaries (mahābhūta) since they have become (bhūta), have occurred, by means of the food, clothing, etc., which are great (mahant) [in importance] because they have to be found every day.

rows.pali: Mahāparihārāni vā bhūtānītipi mahābhūtāni.
rows.english: Or alternatively, they are great primaries (mahābhūta) since they are primaries whose maintenance is great.

rows.pali: Mahāvikāratoti etāni hi anupādinnānipi upādinnānipi mahāvikārāni honti.
rows.english: 102.(d) Great alteration: the unclung-to and the clung-to are the [basis of] great alterations.

rows.pali: Tattha anupādinnānaṃ kappavuṭṭhāne vikāramahattaṃ pākaṭaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Herein, the great alteration of the unclung-to evidences itself in the emergence of an aeon (see XIII.34),

rows.pali: Upādinnānaṃ dhātukkhobhakāle.
rows.english: and that of the clung-to in the disturbance of the elements [in the body].

rows.pali: Tathā hi –
rows.english: For accordingly:

rows.pali: Iti mahāvikārāni bhūtānīti mahābhūtāni.
rows.english: So they are great primaries (mahābhūta) because they have become (bhūta) [the basis of] great (mahant) alteration.

rows.pali: Mahattā bhūtattā cāti etāni hi mahantāni mahatā vāyāmena pariggahetabbattā bhūtāni vijjamānattāti mahattā bhūtattā ca mahābhūtāni.
rows.english: 103.(e) Because they are great and because they are entities: “great” (mahant) because they need great effort to discern them, and “entities” (bhūta = become) because they are existent; thus they are great primaries (mahābhūta) because they are great (mahā) and because they are entities (bhūta).

rows.pali: Evaṃ sabbāpetā dhātuyo mahantapātubhāvādīhi kāraṇehi mahābhūtāni.
rows.english: This is how all these elements are “great primaries” by reason of “great manifestation,” and so on.

rows.pali: Salakkhaṇadhāraṇato pana dukkhādānato ca dukkhādhānato ca sabbāpi dhātulakkhaṇaṃ anatītattā dhātuyo.
rows.english: 104.Again, they are elements (dhātu) because of bearing (dhāraṇa) their own characteristics, because of grasping (ādāna) suffering, and because of sorting out (ādhāna) suffering (see XV.19), and because none of them are exempt from the characteristic of being elements.

rows.pali: Salakkhaṇadhāraṇena ca attano khaṇānurūpadhāraṇena ca dhammā.
rows.english: They are states (dhamma) owing to bearing (dhāraṇa) their own characteristics and owing to their so bearing (dhāraṇa) for the length of the moment appropriate to them.39

rows.pali: Khayaṭṭhena aniccā.
rows.english: They are impermanent in the sense of [liability to] destruction;

rows.pali: Bhayaṭṭhena dukkhā.
rows.english: they are painful in the sense of [causing] terror;

rows.pali: Asārakaṭṭhena anattā.
rows.english: they are not self in the sense of having no core [of permanence, and so on].

rows.pali: Iti sabbāsampi rūpamahābhūtadhātudhammaaniccādivasena ekattanti evaṃ nānattekattato manasikātabbā.
rows.english: Thus there is unity of all since all are materiality, great primaries, elements, states, impermanent, and so on. This is how they should be given attention “as to variety and unity.”

rows.pali: 353.Vinibbhogāvinibbhogatoti sahuppannāva etā ekekasmiṃ sabbapariyantime suddhaṭṭhakādikalāpepi padesena avinibbhuttā.
rows.english: 105. 7. As to resolution (separability) and non-resolution (inseparability): they are positionally unresolvable (inseparable) since they always arise together in every single minimal material group consisting of the bare octad and the others;

rows.pali: Lakkhaṇena pana vinibbhuttāti evaṃ vinibbhogāvinibbhogato manasikātabbā.
rows.english: but they are resolvable (separable) by characteristic. This is how they should be given attention “as to resolution (separability) and non-resolution (inseparability).”

rows.pali: 354.Sabhāgavisabhāgatoti evaṃ avinibbhuttāsu cāpi etāsu purimā dve garukattā sabhāgā.
rows.english: 106. 8. As to the similar and dissimilar: and although they are unresolved (inseparable) in this way, yet the first two similar in heaviness,

rows.pali: Tathā pacchimā lahukattā.
rows.english: and so are the last two in lightness;

rows.pali: Purimā pana pacchimāhi pacchimā ca purimāhi visabhāgāti evaṃ sabhāgavisabhāgato manasikātabbā.
rows.english: but [for this reason] the first two are dissimilar to the last two and the last two to the first two. This is how they should be given attention “as to the similar and dissimilar.”

rows.pali: 355.Ajjhattikabāhiravisesatoti ajjhattikā dhātuyo viññāṇavatthuviññattiindriyānaṃ nissayā honti, sairiyāpathā, catusamuṭṭhānā.
rows.english: 107. 9. As to distinction between internal and external: the internal elements are the [material] support for the physical bases of consciousness, for the kinds of intimation, and for the material faculties. They are associated with postures, and they are of fourfold origination.

rows.pali: Bāhirā vuttaviparītappakārāti evaṃ ajjhattikabāhiravisesato manasikātabbā.
rows.english: The external elements are of the opposite kind. This is how they should be given attention “as to distinction between internal and external.”

rows.pali: 356.Saṅgahatoti kammasamuṭṭhānā pathavīdhātu kammasamuṭṭhānāhi itarāhi ekasaṅgahā hoti samuṭṭhānanānattābhāvato.
rows.english: 108. 10. As to inclusion: kamma-originated earth element is included together with the other kamma-originated elements because there is no difference in their origination.

rows.pali: Tathā cittādisamuṭṭhānā cittādisamuṭṭhānāhīti evaṃ saṅgahato manasikātabbā.
rows.english: Likewise the consciousness-originated is included together with other consciousness-originated elements. This is how they should be given attention “as to inclusion.”

rows.pali: 357.Paccayatoti pathavīdhātu āposaṅgahitā tejoanupālitā vāyovitthambhitā tiṇṇaṃ mahābhūtānaṃ patiṭṭhā hutvā paccayo hoti.
rows.english: 109. 11. As to condition: the earth element, which is held together by water, maintained by fire and distended by air, is a condition for the other three great primaries by acting as their foundation.

rows.pali: Āpodhātu pathavīpatiṭṭhitā tejoanupālitā vāyovitthambhitā tiṇṇaṃ mahābhūtānaṃ ābandhanaṃ hutvā paccayo hoti.
rows.english: The water element, which is founded on earth, maintained by fire and distended by air, is a condition for the other three great primaries by acting as their cohesion.

rows.pali: Tejodhātu pathavīpatiṭṭhitā āposaṅgahitā vāyovitthambhitā tiṇṇaṃ mahābhūtānaṃ paripācanaṃ hutvā paccayo hoti.
rows.english: The fire element, which is founded on earth, held together by water [369] and distended by air, is a condition for the other three great primaries by acting as their maintaining.

rows.pali: Vāyodhātu pathavīpatiṭṭhitā āposaṅgahitā tejoparipācitā tiṇṇaṃ mahābhūtānaṃ vitthambhanaṃ hutvā paccayo hotīti evaṃ paccayato manasikātabbā.
rows.english: The air element, which is founded on earth, held together by water, and maintained by fire, is a condition for the other three great primaries by acting as their distension. This is how they should be given attention “as to condition.”

rows.pali: 358.Asamannāhāratoti pathavīdhātu cettha "ahaṃ pathavīdhātū"ti vā, "tiṇṇaṃ mahābhūtānaṃ patiṭṭhā hutvā paccayo homī"ti vā na jānāti.
rows.english: 110. 12. As to lack of conscious reaction: here too the earth element does not know, “I am the earth element” or “I am a condition by acting as a foundation for three great primaries.” And the other three do not know, “The earth element is a condition for us by acting as a foundation for three great primaries.”

rows.pali: Itarānipi tīṇi "amhākaṃ pathavīdhātu patiṭṭhā hutvā paccayo hotī"ti na jānanti.
rows.english: And the other three do not know, “The earth element is a condition for us by acting as our foundation.”

rows.pali: Esa nayo sabbatthāti evaṃ asamannāhārato manasikātabbā.
rows.english: And similarly in each instance. This is how they should be given attention “as to lack of conscious reaction.”

rows.pali: 359.Paccayavibhāgatoti dhātūnaṃ hi kammaṃ, cittaṃ, āhāro, utūti cattāro paccayā.
rows.english: 111. 13. As to analysis of conditions: there are four conditions for the elements, that is to say, kamma, consciousness, nutriment, and temperature.

rows.pali: Tattha kammasamuṭṭhānānaṃ kammameva paccayo hoti, na cittādayo.
rows.english: Herein, kamma only is the condition for the kamma-originated [elements]; consciousness (citta), etc. [i.e. nutriment and temperature] are not.

rows.pali: Cittādisamuṭṭhānānampi cittādayova paccayā honti, na itare.
rows.english: Consciousness, etc., only are the conditions for the consciousness-originated [elements]; the others are not.

rows.pali: Kammasamuṭṭhānānañca kammaṃ janakapaccayo hoti, sesānaṃ pariyāyato upanissayapaccayo hoti.
rows.english: Kamma is the producing condition40 for the kamma-originated elements; for the rest it is indirectly the decisive-supportive condition.41

rows.pali: Cittasamuṭṭhānānaṃ cittaṃ janakapaccayo hoti, sesānaṃ pacchājātapaccayo atthipaccayo avigatapaccayo ca.
rows.english: Consciousness is the producing condition for the consciousness-originated elements; for the rest it is the post-nascence condition, presence condition and non-disappearance condition.

rows.pali: Āhārasamuṭṭhānānaṃ āhāro janakapaccayo hoti, sesānaṃ āhārapaccayo atthipaccayo avigatapaccayo ca.
rows.english: Nutriment is the producing condition for the nutriment-originated elements; for the rest it is the nutriment condition, presence condition and non-disappearance condition.

rows.pali: Utusamuṭṭhānānaṃ utu janakapaccayo hoti, sesānaṃ atthipaccayo avigatapaccayo ca.
rows.english: Temperature is the productive condition for the temperature-originated elements; for the rest it is the presence condition and non-disappearance condition.

rows.pali: Kammasamuṭṭhānaṃ mahābhūtaṃ kammasamuṭṭhānānampi mahābhūtānaṃ paccayo hoti cittādisamuṭṭhānānampi.
rows.english: Herein, the kamma-originated great primary is the condition for the kamma- originated great primaries, and also for the consciousness-originated [great primaries].

rows.pali: Tathā cittasamuṭṭhānaṃ, āhārasamuṭṭhānaṃ.
rows.english: Likewise are the consciousness-originated [great primary] and the nutriment originated [great primary].

rows.pali: Utusamuṭṭhānaṃ mahābhūtaṃ utusamuṭṭhānānampi mahābhūtānaṃ paccayo hoti kammādisamuṭṭhānānampi.
rows.english: The temperature-originated great primary is the condition for the temperature-originated great primaries, and also for the kamma-originated [great primaries], and so on (cf. XX.27f.).

rows.pali: Tattha kammasamuṭṭhānā pathavīdhātu kammasamuṭṭhānānaṃ itarāsaṃ sahajātaaññamaññanissayaatthiavigatavasena ceva patiṭṭhāvasena ca paccayo hoti, na janakavasena.
rows.english: 112. Herein, the kamma-originated earth element is a condition for the other kamma-originated elements both as conascence, mutuality, support, presence, and non-disappearance conditions and as foundation, but not as producing condition.

rows.pali: Itaresaṃ tisantatimahābhūtānaṃ nissayaatthiavigatavasena paccayo hoti, na patiṭṭhāvasena na janakavasena.
rows.english: It is a condition for the other [three] great primaries in a triple continuity (see XX.22) as support, presence and non-disappearance conditions, but not as foundation or producing condition.

rows.pali: Āpodhātu cettha itarāsaṃ tiṇṇaṃ sahajātādivasena ceva ābandhanavasena ca paccayo hoti, na janakavasena.
rows.english: And here the water element is a condition for the remaining three elements both as conascence, etc., conditions and as cohesion, but not as producing condition.

rows.pali: Itaresaṃ tisantatikānaṃ nissayaatthiavigatapaccayavaseneva, na ābandhanavasena na janakavasena.
rows.english: And for the others in a triple continuity it is a condition as support, presence, and non-disappearance conditions too, but not as cohesion or producing condition.

rows.pali: Tejodhātupettha itarāsaṃ tiṇṇaṃ sahajātādivasena ceva paripācanavasena ca paccayo hoti, na janakavasena.
rows.english: And the fire element here is a condition for the other three elements both as conascence, etc., conditions and as maintaining but not as producing condition.

rows.pali: Itaresaṃ tisantatikānaṃ nissayaatthiavigatapaccayavaseneva, na paripācanavasena, na janakavasena.
rows.english: And for the others in a triple continuity it is a condition as support, presence, and non-disappearance conditions too, but not as maintaining or producing condition.

rows.pali: Vāyodhātupettha itarāsaṃ tiṇṇaṃ sahajātādivasena ceva vitthambhanavasena ca paccayo hoti, na janakavasena.
rows.english: And the air element here is a condition for the other three elements [370] both as conascence, etc., conditions and as distension, but not as producing condition.

rows.pali: Itaresaṃ tisantatikānaṃ nissayaatthiavigatapaccayavaseneva, na vitthambhanavasena, na janakavasena.
rows.english: And for the others in a triple continuity it is a condition as support, presence, and non- disappearance conditions too, but not as distension or producing condition.

rows.pali: Cittaāhārautusamuṭṭhānapathavīdhātuādīsupi eseva nayo.
rows.english: The same method applies in the case of the consciousness-originated, the nutriment-originated, and the temperature-originated earth element, and the rest.

rows.pali: Evaṃ sahajātādipaccayavasappavattāsu ca panetāsu dhātūsu –
rows.english: 113. And when these elements have been made to occur through the influence of the conascence, etc., conditions:

rows.pali: Pathavīādīsu hi ekekaṃ paṭicca itarā tisso tissoti evaṃ ekaṃ paṭicca tisso catudhā sampavattanti.
rows.english: 114. Taking each one, beginning with earth, there are three others whose occurrence is due to that one, thus with three due to one their occurrence takes place in four ways.

rows.pali: Tathā pathavīdhātuādīsu ekekā itarā tisso tisso paṭiccāti evaṃ tisso paṭicca ekā catudhā sampavattati.
rows.english: Likewise each one, beginning with earth, occurs in dependence on the other three, thus with one due to three their occurrence takes place in four ways.

rows.pali: Purimā pana dve paṭicca pacchimā, pacchimā ca dve paṭicca purimā, paṭhamatatiyā paṭicca dutiyacatutthā, dutiyacatutthā paṭicca paṭhamatatiyā, paṭhamacatutthā paṭicca dutiyatatiyā, dutiyatatiyā paṭicca paṭhamacatutthāti evaṃ dve dhātuyo paṭicca dve chadhā sampavattanti.
rows.english: But with the last two dependent on the first two, with the first two dependent on the last two, with the second and fourth dependent on the first and third, with the first and third dependent on the second and fourth, with the second and third dependent on the first and fourth, and with the first and fourth dependent on the second and third, they occur in six ways with two elements due to two.

rows.pali: Tāsu pathavīdhātu abhikkamapaṭikkamādikāle uppīḷanassa paccayo hoti.
rows.english: 115. At the time of moving forward and moving backward (M I 57), the earth element among these is a condition for pressing.

rows.pali: Sāva āpodhātuyā anugatā patiṭṭhāpanassa.
rows.english: That, seconded by the water element, is a condition for establishing on a foundation.

rows.pali: Pathavīdhātuyā pana anugatā āpodhātu avakkhepanassa.
rows.english: But the water element seconded by the earth element is a condition for lowering down.

rows.pali: Vāyodhātuyā anugatā tejodhātu uddharaṇassa.
rows.english: The fire element seconded by the air element is a condition for lifting up.

rows.pali: Tejodhātuyā anugatā vāyodhātu atiharaṇavītiharaṇānaṃ paccayo hotīti evaṃ paccayavibhāgato manasikātabbā.
rows.english: The air element seconded by the fire element is a condition for shifting forwards and shifting sideways (see XX.62f. and M-a I 160). This is how they should be given attention “as to analysis of conditions.”

rows.pali: Evaṃ vacanatthādivasena manasi karontassāpi hi ekekena mukhena dhātuyo pākaṭā honti.
rows.english: 116. As he gives his attention to them “as to word meaning,” etc., in this way, the elements become evident to him under each heading.

rows.pali: Tā punappunaṃ āvajjato manasikaroto vuttanayeneva upacārasamādhi uppajjati.
rows.english: As he again and again adverts and gives attention to them access concentration arises in the way already described.

rows.pali: Svāyaṃ catunnaṃ dhātūnaṃ vavatthāpakassa ñāṇassānubhāvena uppajjanato catudhātuvavatthānantveva saṅkhaṃ gacchati.
rows.english: And this concentration too is called “definition of the four elements” because it arises in one who defines the four elements owing to the influence of his knowledge.

rows.pali: 360.Idañca pana catudhātuvavatthānaṃ anuyutto bhikkhu suññataṃ avagāhati, sattasaññaṃ samugghāteti.
rows.english: 117. This bhikkhu who is devoted to the defining of the four elements immerses himself in voidness and eliminates the perception of living beings.

rows.pali: So sattasaññāya samūhatattā vāḷamigayakkharakkhasādivikappaṃ anāvajjamāno bhayabheravasaho hoti, aratiratisaho, na iṭṭhāniṭṭhesu ugghātanigghātaṃ pāpuṇāti.
rows.english: Since he does not entertain false notions about wild beasts, spirits, ogres, etc., because he has abolished the perception of living beings, he conquers fear and dread and conquers delight and aversion (boredom); he is not exhilarated or depressed42 by agreeable and disagreeable things;

rows.pali: Mahāpañño ca pana hoti amatapariyosāno vā sugatiparāyano vāti.
rows.english: and as one of great understanding, he either ends in the deathless or he is bound for a happy destiny.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ catudhātuvavatthānassa bhāvanāniddeso.
rows.english: This is the description of the development of the defining of the four elements.

rows.pali: 361.Ettāvatā ca yaṃ samādhissa vitthāraṃ bhāvanānayañca dassetuṃ "ko samādhi, kenaṭṭhena samādhī"tiādinā nayena pañhākammaṃ kataṃ, tattha "kathaṃ bhāvetabbo"ti imassa padassa sabbappakārato atthavaṇṇanā samattā hoti.
rows.english: 118.This completes in all its aspects the commentary on the meaning of the clause, “How should it be developed?” in the set of questions beginning with “What is concentration?” which was formulated in order to show the method of development of concentration in detail (see III.1).

rows.pali: Duvidhoyeva hayaṃ idha adhippeto upacārasamādhi ceva appanāsamādhi ca.
rows.english: 119. This concentration as intended here is twofold, that is to say, access concentration and absorption concentration.

rows.pali: Tattha dasasu kammaṭṭhānesu, appanāpubbabhāgacittesu ca ekaggatā upacārasamādhi.
rows.english: Herein, the unification [of mind] in the case of ten meditation subjects and in the consciousness preceding absorption [in the case of the remaining meditation subjects]44 is access concentration.

rows.pali: Avasesakammaṭṭhānesu cittekaggatā appanāsamādhi.
rows.english: The unification of mind in the case of the remaining meditation subjects is absorption concentration.

rows.pali: So duvidhopi tesaṃ kammaṭṭhānānaṃ bhāvitattā bhāvito hoti.
rows.english: And so it is developed in two forms with the development of these meditation subjects.

rows.pali: Tena vuttaṃ "kathaṃ bhāvetabboti imassa padassa sabbappakārato atthavaṇṇanā samattā"ti.
rows.english: Hence it was said above: “This completes in all its aspects the commentary on the meaning of the clause, ‘How should it be developed?’”

rows.pali: 362.Yaṃ pana vuttaṃ "samādhibhāvanāya ko ānisaṃso"ti, tattha diṭṭhadhammasukhavihārādipañcavidho samādhibhāvanāya ānisaṃso.
rows.english: 120. The question, (viii) what are the benefits of the development of concentration? was also asked, however (III.1). Herein, the benefits of the development of concentration are fivefold, as a blissful abiding here and now, and so on.

rows.pali: Tathā hi ye arahanto khīṇāsavā samāpajjitvā ekaggacittā sukhaṃ divasaṃ viharissāmāti samādhiṃ bhāventi, tesaṃ appanāsamādhibhāvanā diṭṭhadhammasukhavihārānisaṃsā hoti.
rows.english: For the development of absorption concentration provides the benefit of a blissful abiding here and now for the Arahants with cankers destroyed who develop concentration, thinking, “We shall attain and dwell with unified mind for a whole day.”

rows.pali: Tenāha bhagavā "na kho panete, cunda, ariyassa vinaye sallekhā vuccanti.
rows.english: Hence the Blessed One said: “But, Cunda, it is not these that are called effacement in the Noble Ones’ discipline;

rows.pali: Diṭṭhadhammasukhavihārā ete ariyassa vinaye vuccantī"ti (ma. ni. 1.82).
rows.english: these are called blissful abidings in the Noble Ones’ discipline” (M I 40).

rows.pali: Sekkhaputhujjanānaṃ samāpattito vuṭṭhāya samāhitena cittena vipassissāmāti bhāvayataṃ vipassanāya padaṭṭhānattā appanāsamādhibhāvanāpi sambādhe okāsādhigamanayena upacārasamādhibhāvanāpi vipassanānisaṃsā hoti.
rows.english: 121. When ordinary people and trainers develop it, thinking, “After emerging, we shall exercise insight with concentrated consciousness,” the development of absorption concentration provides them with the benefit of insight by serving as the proximate cause for insight, and so too does access concentration as a method of arriving at wide open [conditions] in crowded [circumstances].45

rows.pali: Tenāha bhagavā "samādhiṃ, bhikkhave, bhāvetha.
rows.english: Hence the Blessed One said: “Bhikkhus, develop concentration;

rows.pali: Samāhito, bhikkhave, bhikkhu yathābhūtaṃ pajānātī"ti (saṃ. ni. 3.5).
rows.english: a bhikkhu who is concentrated understands correctly” (S III 13).

rows.pali: Ye pana aṭṭha samāpattiyo nibbattetvā abhiññāpādakaṃ jhānaṃ samāpajjitvā samāpattito vuṭṭhāya ekopi hutvā bahudhā hotīti vuttanayā abhiññāyo patthento nibbattenti, tesaṃ sati sati āyatane abhiññāpadaṭṭhānattā appanāsamādhibhāvanā abhiññānisaṃsā hoti.
rows.english: 122. But when they have already produced the eight attainments and then, aspiring to the kinds of direct-knowledge described in the way beginning, “Having been one, he becomes many” (XII.2), they produce them by entering upon jhāna as the basis for direct-knowledge and emerging from it, then the development of absorption concentration provides for them the benefit of the kinds of direct-knowledge, since it becomes the proximate cause for the kinds of direct-knowledge whenever there is an occasion.

rows.pali: Tenāha bhagavā – "so yassa yassa abhiññāsacchikaraṇīyassa dhammassa cittaṃ abhininnāmeti abhiññāsacchikiriyāya, tatra tatreva sakkhibhabbataṃ pāpuṇāti sati sati āyatane"ti (ma. ni. 3.158; a. ni. 3.102).
rows.english: Hence the Blessed One said: “He attains the ability to be a witness, through realization by direct-knowledge, of any state realizable by direct-knowledge to which his mind inclines, whenever there is an occasion” (M III 96; A I 254).

rows.pali: Ye aparihīnajjhānā brahmaloke nibbattissāmāti brahmalokūpapattiṃ patthentā apatthayamānā vāpi puthujjanā samādhito na parihāyanti, tesaṃ bhavavisesāvahattā appanāsamādhibhāvanā bhavavisesānisaṃsā hoti.
rows.english: 123. When ordinary people have not lost their jhāna, and they aspire to rebirth in the Brahmā-world thus, “Let us be reborn in the Brahmā-world,” or even though they do not make the actual aspiration, then the development of absorption concentration provides them with the benefits of an improved form of existence since it ensures that for them.

rows.pali: Tenāha bhagavā – "paṭhamaṃ jhānaṃ parittaṃ bhāvetvā kattha upapajjanti.
rows.english: Hence the Blessed One said: “Where do they reappear after developing the first jhāna limitedly?

rows.pali: Brahmapārisajjānaṃ devānaṃ sahabyataṃ upapajjantī"tiādi (vibha. 1024).
rows.english: They reappear in the company of the deities of Brahmā’s Retinue” (Vibh 424), and so on.

rows.pali: Upacārasamādhibhāvanāpi pana kāmāvacarasugatibhavavisesaṃ āvahatiyeva.
rows.english: And even the development of access concentration ensures an improved form of existence in the happy destinies of the sensual sphere.

rows.pali: Ye pana ariyā aṭṭha samāpattiyo nibbattetvā nirodhasamāpattiṃ samāpajjitvā satta divasāni acittā hutvā diṭṭheva dhamme nirodhaṃ nibbānaṃ patvā sukhaṃ viharissāmāti samādhiṃ bhāventi, tesaṃ appanāsamādhibhāvanā nirodhānisaṃsā hoti.
rows.english: 124. But when Noble Ones who have already produced the eight attainments develop concentration, thinking, “We shall enter upon the attainment of cessation, and by being without consciousness for seven days we shall abide in bliss here and now by reaching the cessation that is Nibbāna,” then the development of absorption concentration provides for them the benefit of cessation.

rows.pali: Tenāha – "soḷasahi ñāṇacariyāhi navahi samādhicariyāhi vasībhāvatā paññā nirodhasamāpattiyā ñāṇa"nti (paṭi. ma. 1.34).
rows.english: Hence it is said: “Understanding as mastery owing to … sixteen kinds of behaviour of knowledge, and to nine kinds of behaviour of concentration, is knowledge of the attainment of cessation” (Paṭis I 97; see Ch. XXIII, 18f.).

rows.pali: Evamayaṃ diṭṭhadhammasukhavihārādi pañcavidho samādhibhāvanāya ānisaṃso –
rows.english: 125. That is how this benefit of the development of concentration is fivefold as a blissful abiding here and now, and so on.

rows.pali: 363.Ettāvatā ca "sīle patiṭṭhāya naro sapañño"ti imissā gāthāya sīlasamādhipaññāmukhena desite visuddhimagge samādhipi paridīpito hoti.
rows.english: 126. And at this point in the Path of Purification, which is taught under the headings of virtue, concentration and understanding in the stanza, “When a wise man, established well in virtue …,” concentration has been fully explained.

rows.pali: 364.Paṭhamo sīlaniddeso.
Dutiyo dhutaṅganiddeso;.
Tatiyo kammaṭṭhānaggahaṇaniddeso.
Catuttho pathavīkasiṇaniddeso.
Pañcamo sesakasiṇaniddeso.
Chaṭṭho asubhaniddeso.
Sattamo chaanussatiniddeso.
Aṭṭhamo sesānussatiniddeso.
Navamo brahmavihāraniddeso.
Dasamo āruppaniddeso.
Paṭikkūlasaññādhātuvavatthānadvayaniddeso ekādasamoti.
294.Idāni āruppānantaraṃ ekā saññāti evaṃ uddiṭṭhāya āhāre paṭikkūlasaññāya bhāvanāniddeso anuppatto.
rows.english: 1. Now comes the description of the development of the perception of repulsiveness in nutriment, which was listed as the “one perception”1 next to the immaterial states (III.105).

rows.pali: Tattha āharatīti āhāro.
rows.english: Herein, it nourishes (āharati, lit. “brings on”), thus it is nutriment (āhāra, lit. “bringing on”).

rows.pali: So catubbidho kabaḷīkārāhāro, phassāhāro, manosañcetanāhāro, viññāṇāhāroti.
rows.english: That is of four kinds as: physical nutriment, nutriment consisting of contact, nutriment consisting of mental volition, and nutriment consisting of consciousness.2

rows.pali: Ko panettha kimāharatīti?
rows.english: 2. But what is it here that nourishes (brings on) what?

rows.pali: Kabaḷīkārāhāro ojaṭṭhamakaṃ rūpaṃ āharati.
rows.english: Physical nutriment (kabaliṅkārāhāra) nourishes (brings on) the materiality of the octad that has nutritive essence as eighth:3

rows.pali: Phassāhāro tisso vedanā āharati.
rows.english: contact as nutriment nourishes (brings on) the three kinds of feeling;

rows.pali: Manosañcetanāhāro tīsu bhavesu paṭisandhiṃ āharati.
rows.english: mental volition as nutriment nourishes (brings on) rebirth- linking in the three kinds of becoming;

rows.pali: Viññāṇāhāro paṭisandhikkhaṇe nāmarūpaṃ āharati.
rows.english: consciousness as nutriment nourishes (brings on) mentality-materiality at the moment of rebirth-linking.

rows.pali: Tesu kabaḷīkārāhāre nikantibhayaṃ.
rows.english: 3. Now, when there is physical nutriment there is attachment, which brings peril;

rows.pali: Phassāhāre upagamanabhayaṃ.
rows.english: when there is nutriment as contact there is approaching, which brings peril;

rows.pali: Manosañcetanāhāre upapattibhayaṃ.
rows.english: when there is nutriment as mental volition there is rebirth-linking, which brings peril.4

rows.pali: Viññāṇāhāre paṭisandhibhayaṃ.
Evaṃ sappaṭibhayesu ca tesu kabaḷīkārāhāro puttamaṃsūpamena (saṃ. ni. 2.63) dīpetabbo.
rows.english: And to show how they bring fear thus, physical nutriment should be illustrated by the simile of the child’s flesh (S II 98),

rows.pali: Phassāhāro niccammagāvūpamena (saṃ. ni. 2.63).
rows.english: contact as nutriment by the simile of the hideless cow (S II 99),

rows.pali: Manosañcetanāhāro aṅgārakāsūpamena (saṃ. ni. 2.63).
rows.english: mental volition as nutriment by the simile of the pit of live coals (S II 99),

rows.pali: Viññāṇāhāro sattisatūpamenāti (saṃ. ni. 2.63).
rows.english: and consciousness as nutriment by the simile of the hundred spears (S II 100).

rows.pali: Imesu pana catūsu āhāresu asitapītakhāyitasāyitappabhedo kabaḷīkāro āhārova imasmiṃ atthe āhāroti adhippeto.
rows.english: 4.But of these four kinds of nutriment it is only physical nutriment, classed as what is eaten, drunk, chewed, and tasted, that is intended here as “nutriment” in this sense.

rows.pali: Tasmiṃ āhāre paṭikkūlākāraggahaṇavasena uppannā saññā āhāre paṭikkūlasaññā.
rows.english: The perception arisen as the apprehension of the repulsive aspect in that nutriment is, “perception of repulsiveness in nutriment.”

rows.pali: Taṃ āhāre paṭikkūlasaññaṃ bhāvetukāmena kammaṭṭhānaṃ uggahetvā uggahato ekapadampi avirajjhantena rahogatena paṭisallīnena asitapītakhāyitasāyitappabhede kabaḷīkārāhāre dasahākārehi paṭikkūlatā paccavekkhitabbā.
rows.english: 5. One who wants to develop that perception of repulsiveness in nutriment should learn the meditation subject and see that he has no uncertainty about even a single word of what he has learnt. Then he should go into solitary retreat and review repulsiveness in ten aspects in the physical nutriment

rows.pali: Seyyathidaṃ, gamanato, pariyesanato, paribhogato, āsayato, nidhānato, aparipakkato, paripakkato, phalato, nissandato, sammakkhanatoti.
rows.english: classified as what is eaten, drunk, chewed, and tasted, that is to say, as to going, seeking, using, secretion, receptacle, what is uncooked (undigested), what is cooked (digested), fruit, outflow, and smearing.

rows.pali: 295.Tattha gamanatoti evaṃ mahānubhāve nāma sāsane pabbajitena sakalarattiṃ buddhavacanasajjhāyaṃ vā samaṇadhammaṃ vā katvā kālasseva vuṭṭhāya cetiyaṅgaṇabodhiyaṅgaṇavattaṃ katvā pānīyaṃ paribhojanīyaṃ upaṭṭhapetvā pariveṇaṃ sammajjitvā sarīraṃ paṭijaggitvā āsanamārūyha vīsatiṃsa vāre kammaṭṭhānaṃ manasikaritvā uṭṭhāya pattacīvaraṃ gahetvā nijanasambādhāni pavivekasukhāni chāyūdakasampannāni sucīni sītalāni ramaṇīyabhūmibhāgāni tapovanāni pahāya ariyaṃ vivekaratiṃ anapekkhitvā susānābhimukhena siṅgālena viya āhāratthāya gāmābhimukhena gantabbaṃ.
rows.english: 6. 1. Herein, as to going: even when a man has gone forth in so mighty a dispensation, still after he has perhaps spent all night reciting the Enlightened One’s word or doing the ascetic’ s work, after he has risen early to do the duties connected with the shrine terrace and the Enlightenment-tree terrace, to set out the water for drinking and washing, to sweep the grounds and to see to the needs of the body, after he has sat down on his seat and given attention to his meditation subject twenty or thirty times5 and got up again, then he must take his bowl and [outer] robe, he must leave behind the ascetics’ woods that are not crowded with people, offer the bliss of seclusion, possess shade and water, and are clean, cool, delightful places, he must disregard the Noble Ones’ delight in seclusion, and he must set out for the village in order to get nutriment, as a jackal for the charnel ground.

rows.pali: Evaṃ gacchatā ca mañcamhā vā pīṭhamhā vā otaraṇato paṭṭhāya pādarajagharagolikavaccādisamparikiṇṇaṃ paccattharaṇaṃ akkamitabbaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 7.And as he goes thus, from the time when he steps down from his bed or chair he has to tread on a carpet6 covered with the dust of his feet, geckos’ droppings, and so on.

rows.pali: Tato appekadā mūsikajatukavaccādīhi upahatattā antogabbhato paṭikkūlataraṃ pamukhaṃ daṭṭhabbaṃ hoti.
